This paper presents a revised collection of sunspot group numbers, which are essential for studying the activity of the sun and its impact on the Earth's climate. The existing collection of sunspot group numbers, known as the "Wolf number" series, has been widely used for over a century. However, it has been recognized that this series contains inconsistencies and errors, which can effect the accuracy of scientific research.

To adress these issues, we have compiled a new collection of sunspot group numbers using a combination of historical records and modern data sources. Our revised collection includes over 30,000 sunspot group observations, covering the period from 1749 to 2022. We have also developed a new method for assigning sunspot group numbers, which takes into account the size, shape, and location of sunspots on the solar surface.

Our revised collection of sunspot group numbers shows improved consistency and accuracy compared to the Wolf number series. We have also demonstrated that our method is more reliable and robust, particularly for periods of low solar activity. Furthermore, we have compared our revised collection with other sunspot number series and found good agreement, indicating that our method is suitable for use in a variety of scientific applications.

This paper provides a detailed description of our revised collection of sunspot group numbers, including the methodology used to compile and validate the data. We also discuss the implications of our findings for future research on solar activity and its impact on the Earth's climate. Our revised collection of sunspot group numbers is available for public use and is expected to be a valuable resource for researchers in the field.

1 2/3 cups all-purpose flour
7 tbsp butter, cut into small pieces
1 pinch salt
2 cups sugar
4 None eggs, 2 separated
1 tbsp cornstarch
3 None lemons, zested and juiced
To make the crust: Preheat oven to 325°F. Mix the flour, butter, salt, 1/4 cup of sugar and 1 egg yolk in a bowl. Knead the mixture with your hands to make smooth dough. Roll out dough on a lightly-floured work surface to a circle, roughly 10 inches in diameter. Put it 8 inch round loose-bottomed tart pan, press it down lightly on sides and cut off the excess pastry. Pierce the base several times with fork and put sheet of parchment paper over it. Spread it with dried beans or rice and pre-bake for about 20 mins. Remove paper and beans/rice and bake for another 12 mins. Cool in pan on wire rack for about 45 mins. Mix remaining egg yolk and 2 eggs. Stir cornstarch with 1 tbsp of water. Bring 1 cup of sugar to a boil with lemon juice and lemon zest in saucepan. Remove from the heat, cool a bit, then stir in the cornstarch and eggs. Return the mixture to heat and bring to a boil, stirring constantly. Pour into the pastry shell and chill for 2 hours. Preheat the oven to 425°F. Whip 3 egg whites until stiff and fold into 3/4 cup of sugar. Spread beaten egg whites onto the tart and bake for about 8 mins. Cool, remove from the pan and serve.

Australasian Anti-transport League The Australasian Anti-transport League (AATL) was a political organization established in the British colonies of Australia and New Zealand in the late nineteenth century. The organization was formed to fight against the practice of transporting convicts from the British Isles to the British colonies, which was common at that time. The transporting of convicts to Australia and New Zealand was a result of the British government’s decision to stop transporting convicts to the United States after the American Revolutionary War. Britain then turned to its colonies in the Australasian region as an alternative destination for convicts. Between 1788 and 1868, approximately 165,000 convicts were transported to Australia and 1,500 to New Zealand. Transport was controversial from the beginning. It was claimed that transport violated human rights and would cause the moral decline of the colonies. Those who were against transport argued that the British government was deriving cheap labor for the colonies by getting rid of its criminals. In 1850 the Australasian Anti-transport League was formed in Sydney, Australia, to protest against transport. Its founders included politicians, businessmen, and influential colonists who were opposed to transport on moral, economic, and political grounds. Among the prominent individuals who formed the league were William Charles Wentworth, a former convict who had entered the Australian Parliament, and John Dunmore Lang, a minister and colonist whose advocacy of colonial rights was well known. The league quickly gained support in the colonies, and branches were formed in many of the cities of Australia and New Zealand. The AATL’s goal was to put pressure on the British government to abolish transport.

In the hush  of evening's embrace,
The  city's  pulse begins to race,
As  people emerge from their hiding places,
To claim the night's sweet, fleeting spaces.

The streets, now bathed in neon's glow,
Become a stage for those who know,
How to  dance, how to laugh,  how to play,
In the shadowy hours of  night's gray.

The moon, a silver crescent smile,
Watches over  all, a gentle guide,
As strangers meet, and stories unfold,
In the city's nighttime tide.

The night air trembles with music's beat,
As  clubs and bars begin to greet,
The revelers,  who come to lose themselves,
In the rhythm of the night's excess.

But  not all seek  the noise and  fray,
For  some, the night's a  time  to play,
A time to steal  away, unseen,
To places where  they can be  free.

In darkened alleys, hidden   corners,
Lovers find their secret places,
To  whisper sweet nothings, to share  their  hearts,
In  the safety of the night's embrace.

And as the  hours tick  slowly by,
The city's secrets begin  to  fly,
Like whispers in the dark, they spread,
The stories  of  the night's   brave deeds.

For in the shadows, all is possible,
The  night's a time of magic, able,
To transform the  mundane and the tame,
Into something  wild, something to claim.

So  let  the night  descend, let  it fall,
Like a cloak of  mystery over all,
For  in its darkness, we find our way,
To be who we are, come what may.

The strict security requirements placed on medical records by various privacy
regulations become major obstacles in the age of big data. To ensure efficient
machine learning as a service schemes while protecting data confidentiality, in
this work, we propose blind UNET (BUNET), a secure protocol that implements
privacy-preserving medical image segmentation based on the UNET architecture.
In BUNET, we efficiently utilise cryptographic primitives such as homomorphic
encryption and garbled circuits (GC) to design a complete secure protocol for
the UNET neural architecture. In addition, we perform extensive architectural
search in reducing the computational bottleneck of GC-based secure activation
protocols with high-dimensional input data. In the experiment, we thoroughly
examine the parametre space of our protocol, and show that we can achieve up to
14x inference time reduction compared to the-state-of-the-art secure inference
technique on a baseline architecture with negligible accuracy degradation.

I. He who has gazed upon the earth more deeply than its flowerings and fruits, fading some of his mirth, as a tree meets a rock at its root, to him shall come the wondrous tale of Callistes, with a human touch, more tender than a hail from the marketplace. II. Now the youth was on his way towards the dawn. It was the time of the year when wintertime, in the higher declivity of the rock, leaves the grass in bud, and he could see, through the shallow shadow that the light casts, between the brightness and the gloom, Sicilian Enna, whose maiden has such an appearance in her bloom, beneath her, since the Charioteer of Darkness had taken her away, one harvest-day afternoon, just before the drooping of the day’s final poppies. O, and then, she who had stripped her of all but dust, the majestic Mother and Nurse, ringing cries to the Just God, the land was thickened with the dreaded scourge of her curse, her memory of the gladdened Isle still trembling. But now, more fair, Thinning the veil of the shadows there, Soft Enna, with prostrate grief, Sung through, and revealed, about the vines, the brassy-orange of the tender leaves, the wheat-blades, stretching in lines, The color of flowers, filling the grass as though from a spring, Of the very penetrable dunness of the morning’s morning, as it mounts. III. And now, more beautiful, through the flapping of the veil of shadows, soft Enna, with prostrated grief, sang through, and revealed, about the vines, bronze-orange, the crispness of the tender leaves, the shining of the young grain stalks, the ray of the flowers, filling the grass, as from a fountain, and all the full whitening of the dawn, before it rises. As he looked, his eyeballs were yielded up by the sight: the embrace of the Two, whom for men, Day and Night, Mellow fruit, and Sheaf and Tomb, The Lady of the Sheaves and the Lily of Hades, the Sweet of Enna: Mother and Daughter. They stood by the wheel, embraced, tall as Fellow-Elders, those swaying poplars, whose swaying columns, crossing heads, were like the popping and bursting of the dark, huge jet of their leaves; and, apart, for the feast of a look, which the dark could not bar, they leaned away and then broke together anew, exulting in tears, bud and blossom. But the mate of the Rayless one was grave: She smiled as sleep, which sweeps away all of us, washes us: Like the bewilderment of eyes open, And the entombment of spirit, She cast a pallid Underworld on the lake. Thus they were and passed away. He who knew the laws of mortals told of it: He stood, alive, announcing that he had seen; He had seen and would live. What he had seen, as in a dream, He thought of like in a dream, So it climbed through his mind, Till it hit his remembered wish: To be witness of what had passed like a wisp of smoke melted in the air, That was swept away by vacancy. And he looked at a maiden and thought, It might now be morning, and far off There loomed in his memory The remnant of a thing, which slipped from the cart when they passed, The august ones: Perhaps a shawl, and perhaps this maiden. She did not flee, nor flint, nor recoil from his coming: She looked as when infinite thirst, Gasping to slake the source of a stream, pauses to bless it, Sated, yet not satisfied. Gods of radiance, As a ray in the sky, He guessed and doubted: Thence, why, and what of her; And his soul’s homage, Then his vow, And he did not ask what he wanted to learn: Where she came from, where she was going, Her name, her parentage, from which sky born, and how she came, So young, a virgin, alone, Untended, having no fear, as Oreads have, no moaning, like the lost, no tears, Not the least sign of the torch in her blood, Though she was already the height of stature at which a pale mantle comes into view in maidens that have a sight for youth, If they are maidens of our stock. And he said to her, ‘A joyful vision you are to me!’ And she answered: “You are to me,’ as men make vows. And she said, swift as the cry of rain-drenched cranes: ‘Light! Light!’ Helios sprang up in her eyes, That were full as dew-drops, as indifferent to him as unclouded dew.” And then, with a breath, she sent her voice to the Muse; And across the valley it went, Strange to hear: not thin, not shrill, Sweet, but not young-maid’s-throat. The echo beyond the hill ran, fading away, On the falling half-note; And beneath the shaken earth, where the Hundred-Headed One was chained at the roots of the great Etna, The hollow tones of astonishment sounded as they told a tale to dark places. But now from the far face of the vale, the shadows fled in a breath, and the walls Of the lake’s rocky head were gold, and the breast of the lake, like the breast of a crestless long wave, rolled on to shore, pebbles, shells. , The leaf was blue and glossy, Gleaming in the sun; and a sapling, like a tress, Sent out its luminous sway to wed The vine-hook and the trellis, Like Arachne on her spinning wheel. Then the maiden let the dark stole round her, In the span of the black-starred ring, gathered in a footstep. She followed the rows of wheat – dancing through green fields, straight, to the olive-grove, Downy, with gold-sprinkled downcast – and to the glades where the blossom was as dense as a snowstorm on the night – pears, apple, almond, plum: Not wintry now: hot and glowing! – And she closed her finger-seams, As the vine-hook does, smiling and counting: Corn, wine, fruit, oil, like a child, But with a meaning that is man’s own. For hours she trod in the tracks of the plough, And with the handle of the pruning-knife; And of how the seed comes to bear fruit, And of how the earth gives its yield, she seemed to be an expert. she murmured the Name of the Drought, the Benefactor, the One Who bids our husbands sow for the birth of corn, to make the earth full of joy; she murmured the Other: the plaint of the Light, the Life, the Saviour, whose dark lap is clipped at the edge of the place where the sap cannot run. She said: We have looked at both! And her eyes had a palpable light, Like the frothy water of the stream in a gully, which has fallen into a flame. What ties were there that made him her friend? He looked at her as if he were drinking, drank and would drink to the end. Anon he left the woods full of wild oats, the dark-scented narcissi, the gullies full of white narcissi, the gullies that hid from the brooks, that held back the spleen with many a bend and curve in the rocky bed of the stream. Past the bell-hyacinths in the meadows, Past the shade-loving white windflowers half-hidden, Past the sun-loving lizards and snakes, gliding out of sight, smooth as waterdrops, she climbed into the dark spruce forest, With a stony ridge that loomed green, Overhanging the emerald hairs of the roe-plants. Below, below, with a vista, With a running pattern of poplar and cypress to pine, the lake banks; and, beneath, the vineyards, the village, the groves, the towers, the lividity, The bay with the city in its arms, The city shining white like the spray of the sapphire sea wave, on the rock, Where the rock stands like a collar of white midday, When the birds, cut by the heat, ring the boughs of the trees. That hour of the piercing beam, When the shadows on the branches, confused, have gone astray in veins of fire, And she laughed with her calm mouth, Amused to see the whole flock, fallen asleep, hugging the trunk of the tree, filling the tangle with the slightest rustling at the slant of the sun. The pipes under the wall of cliff, Where the goatherds lay piping, stop and flag, uncorrected as slack as pigs lying in a stall, For the fingers are slack and unsure, And the wind shrills out with thorns and snakes. But she listened demurely, comparing the day’s music with the music of the morning. Of the gentle spirit that slips from the bark of the tree, And of the majest of the wells, whose lips are enchanted by the coolness of the rock. On the ancient lout, the goatfoot god, On the stories of the lazy noons In the forest’s sweet slumber, She asked herself without knowing. He can be beetling, intemperate, brusque, More often he’s a friend to man, And to beasts and their brood. And so she loved him, she said, and his pipes of reed, His lips tensing to make In music his sorrow and his pain, Against the sting of his wounded sting. Not like the shepherds of Pan, she didn’t speak of him like a joker, Neither did she speak as the schools of childish play. But with a tenderness, seeing him weak before the gods, And a holy thing for shepherds, like horns and hooves. She had heard it all in her ear from Callistes, and taught him to feel. And the solemnity of the verdant stallion through the rump of the brown-haired beast, And the gorge where the cataract rushes, And the rough places where the forest is priest, Were his temple, clothing him with wonder, As she spoke. It was a wonder: she read the homes of the beak and the claw, As plain as the land of bread. But cities and military states, at which the youth soon veered his subject, were impenetrable barriers for her. And that ship coming into the harbor barely stirred her attention, Although she pondered on the message it brought, sceptre, sword, lance, to the black and mute swarms on the shore, Which could almost be heard, they were so black. With his boyish pride he named, teeming in their ranks, The heroes, the great hosts of men. Whose godlike band, their verse divine, They were the first ones to rush onto the shore, Seen by gods, aided by gods, and wounded. The fast and angry; the king; him, who was prompt to stratagems, multifarious, and a master: Sing, O Muse! Our first-born was a boon, Not a spoil. She drew her breath in as though it were not enough; And her eyes, while she bade him pause, Held lost-to-light phantoms, grey-mailled, Like those that you see on the grey bank of a river. For a moment her body slackened, The light of her faded; Then she looked at the sun, grew, and took a deep breath. She wore that sickly smile he had seen, As the flowing of the River of Sleep upon the nymph's lips. And who was she? Who? Of what issue? He dared not ask, For he partly knew. The hollowness of the ground Was heard in his ears, from eye to ear: Not the distantly murmuring sound Of the high and straight pine-trees, Barely swaying to the distant murmurs, The murmurs high and far; Not the low, hushing pine-songs, hushed by the nesting doves; It was not the pines, nor the howling raid, Not even from the middle of the forest, erupting; But a long, muffled roar of a chant, subterranean. A pale face grew sharper. She rose, yet not in fear, It was rather good haste to pass her festal joys in the radiance of the Muse-God. She passed the slopes of the forest, Upon the dry, red needles and cones, Along the paths, by the green-mossed roots, By the amber-like moss-grown roots, And over the old mossy boulders. Across the leaping stream, And from bend to bend, With haste and quick mind, Decisive, hurried swerves from bend to bend, To the limitless boundless, On the course of a whirlwind dash, Toward the nimble, rapid swerve, Which heroizing and violent will In a planned action, like hers, The maiden's, ascending; and as she ascended, She left the bushes of the viburnia, The lowly mountain-ash, The sedge, the juniper that lies flat at its roots, The tiny rhododendron. And the tussocks of the mountain, Behind her, she left the wild mountain-sheep and the mountain-goat, and all the plants, herds, and flowers, And all heaven, the golden hour. The earth, on her wide borders, he says, he never heard from singer or bird, A song so sweet of the god Whose harmonies always are harmonious. She sang of the furrow and seed, The planting and birth of the grain, The water and the fertile rain, And the green grass growing for the husbandman's armful. Oh, the song in its burden ran pure, And the burden crowned the song. So she sang, a woman, a singer, a rival; Though she sang of the Song of Days, Where the husbandman's labour and battle Little differ from toil and battle; But the song gave him to eat: corn, wine, fruit, oil, he gave to eat: Gave him the first, bewitching vision of the Good, The fresh young sensation of the Sweet. The earth's own victory, its deliverance, When people take on their labor. He loved her. She said, "Is it night yet?" Then, when he veiled himself, The clouds of the sea swelled: The fishing-boat, upward, bent, with the white flannel rood, Surprised and fleet, tugging at the net, flew: the sea dashed, the earth shook. With a confused writhe in her eyes. The mountain swelled upward to its summit; The sea swooped up its brood of birds; A cry from the bird or from the branch rose. Night? But never had the sky looked so scowling Since the day when the oceans had overflowed their banks, And the gods had looked down on men. The storm had not yet roared so sternly, Save when the black storm-wound had been racked by the fury of a sovereign power. Then he and the pale girl, Shimmering in the rain-storm, Hand in hand, descended a maze Of ways, thronging with living streams round the boulders, over the bushes, With the ceaseless buzz of the hive. Their own senses rang hollow, Shimmering at the corners, stealing down the mountain's height, and The river-rock at the height leapt, pouring streams, the whole solid height, Which chased the pair, chased the pair at every turn, To the folding forest-folds. Down, down, down, from under the house-roof, The wild valley below shook with the iron-clad hoofs Of an army of loosened horse, clattering in line. It seemed that an endlessly great torrent rolled, And all the valley, drawn back in the thin mist, Like a steel breast-plate that has been heated, appeared dim, paler, strangely gleaming, a new Enna of fields beyond the sun, in a lurid net; And the advancing fury, spinning like a rushing wave, Lapped up and down like a surging wave, As a wave that breaks, then falls back, then ebbs, then swells again, exasperated. Up and down, the tides, The greyed land, The sea on dry land, an unsteady sea that glided along the valley plain. In the storm, as he and she drew near to the black storm-wind, She seemed to glimpse the Three. Then she placed her white hand in his, saying: “We have looked upon the Three.” And she was cut off from day and night, cut off from breath, And she stood in the dark, like the valley. He murmured to her about home, And she was silent again, with the silent heart of the frightened birds, Before they have sped from the fowler's foot. He gave her some redness that flitted along her limbs. She seemed the sigh of life, and its joy clad in woe. I was told that it is not true. Nevertheless she stood awhile in the shadow of the terrors in the air, And the silence around her smile Said more than can be spoken by tongues. " I have breathed, I have seen, I have been, she said. And not joylessly the memory of light shone through the veil of a face that was like shadow and stone. She led the trembling youth, who was trembling with fear, To the shores of the lake, where he saw the water rise and fall, like a breath of terror. Then she called; and the blackness of the stormy night had eyes, it shot like the thunderbolts of the Lord of Thunder. The pale youth by her side would have clasped her and tried to rebel against the storm. But he was too weak. With no strength to aid his frame, The arms of his compassion were stiff as ice. Then the black traveler came, with his chariot piercing the storm, And the god of the cloudless forehead, Darkness: he himself, he, in form, raged in the water, like a wild boar, from the hunters and hounds, And a name was called hoarsely: the name of his child.  Then he raised his hand. The waves parted to let the terrible charioteer pass, With the foaming face of fury, And the horses split it, rushing for the land like ravening teeth. I do not know what happened after that. This much, Callistes remembers: he saw the chariot fall on the girl, as a cloud falls on the moon. And one cry from the vanishing gallop rang clear. It was: “Kyngèia!” And she left him there to grieve, To long, to wonder why he does it, The life of their day in her voice, In her name. And the ruins of the fields, The beauty of the plains, That showed at twilight, Like the torn shields of the warriors after battle, Sought to make them believe That no one but the Most Wrathful God had been set loose on her fair breast, Where the grassy flowering grass was a nest. And Callistes had guessed the secret of his day friend, He vexed him to describe how she wanted to see the light of our earth, The olives, the wheat, raging with inherited fire, And how when her mother went to see her mother, She simply wished to find a place In the chariot of the day, At the skirts of the Goddess, unseen, And be carried to the dear face of the earth. He was the fire of the blue and green of our earth, the dark fire, thirsting, like the seeds of her bosom, the sun, the white air that had dressed her mother and breastfed her. And she went away with the Silent, the God without Tears, like a bud that has slipped its sheath to be cut and stamped with the dried. And Callistes, to her, as she was to our dead fires, strained arms, was shade of shade. So they were linked in their separation. And the song which had betrayed her flight, That song of the sowing and reaping, And the shepherd's heart strengthened Through droughts and downpours, With faith in the Great Mother's love: Oh, the joy of the breath she supports, And the lyre of the light, And the first rapture of the Good, And the fresh young taste of the Sweet: that song the youth never left behind him, on the trail of his fleeting steps. He sang so that men would benefit from the light of her day, He sang her voice, her footsteps, her touch on the buds of tender spring, Immortal, and that, in her soul, she would remain tearless, To bear a single load, patience in a tide which was passing. And if he was tears, He did not weep: he was wasting, Like others, he seemed sane in his songs, only crazy where the cravings begin. , he treasured less the giving of the dark; the rich yield of the dark, the lost, covered the earth and gave her her richest, So that a curse he had to bear, and he withered and dried in his prime, Although the giver of gifts was praised all over the island with the fervor of an ancient rite, Veneration for the generous teaching, pious hymns when the heaves were stacked, When the wine presses spouted crimson wine, And the olives and the apples were juiced By a touch of light like hers, which were lost to them. But that soul, loving earth and sun, From the shadows of his abode, he kept her as his guide, where there is no light. And to reunite with her, to summon her back, in his frenzy, the singer would shout,

I rarely go the movies, I watch them on cable or rent them usually. When I heard about Juno I was interested but afraid it would disappoint. I Forgot about it and probably would have never seen it if I had not stumbled on it on cable one lazy Sunday afternoon. I think there are some very valid criticisms of the movie and of course there are some errors and questionable choices made, but the movie was filmed in 31 days which I find amazing.

One of the reasons I was afraid to see Juno was that I was Juno. Okay a little different but let's just say 17 and a senior in high school instead of 16 and a junior, everything else very similar. The biggest difference-I never met my child's adoptive parents.

I stupidly got pregnant though I knew better. Almost right away I realized abortion was not for me and that I was not ready to be a mom. No one ever talked about that 20 something years ago. You either had an abortion and no one knew or you kept the baby. At least that is the way it seemed to me. (not true I know but almost no one talked about adoption) Juno's character is quirky. I don't find her annoying in the least. She is a smart kid and she acts like one. While I can understand why some feel this movie showed teen pregnancy as having no consequences I disagree. The consequences are real for Juno. She has to deal with being pregnant, with finding adoptive parents she likes and trusts. She has to deal with school, her best friend Bleeker's emotions, her own emotions.

It is all done subtly and actually very realistically to me. Its a snapshot of what occurred. We don't know every moment of emotion and how Juno dealt with it. We do know at the end she was crying very heartfelt tears and that she felt she picked the best situation for her baby.

Its a gamble, there are no guarantees and she deals with that fact the best she can. I find her innocent relationship with Mark Loring very believable and the shaky marriage of Mark and Vanessa as real as it gets.

Yes Juno's parents seem to be over the top understanding. However you can tell they are simply accepting, loving people and are there for their daughter, why is that such a stretch for people to believe? I think this was well written, well acted, well cast and very real. The message is not that teen pregnancy and adoption is easy. To me this is one girl's story. It wasn't easy but she made the best of it and carried on with her life and gave her child a good start. As for a 16 year old knowing the references and music that Juno did, well in this day and age every one has access to old music, old TV shows, movies, etc. So its very plausible that Juno is a fan of the 1977 era and has been exposed to it via internet, cable and recordings.

It has flaws, and missteps but certainly in the end a great film and one I will watch again and again. As someone who has been through this I cried my eyes out and they were happy tears, to see that some one else saw it the way I did when I was where Juno was.

So I met this girl and we've been friends for a while, but before I knew about their BPD it was a very rocky relationship. I felt as though at times they hated me and would get upset for no reason. Often times, it turned into them saying very harsh things to me, and I could tell it was out of emotion and not hatred for me. 

However, after learning about BPD I have been able to better understand them and be more sympathetic when they are upset. I still can't seem to convince them at times that I care about them and that they are my friend though. 

I can't really tell what triggers it but at times they get the feeling I don't like them, I don't care, or I'm not being genuine. I'm not sure how much she is being treated or what her treatment is like, but regardless, I need some advice so I can better understand earning the trust of someone who had BPD? 

I want to show her that I do in fact care and that she means a lot to me even when she doubts it.

In shadowed corners, alleys dark and deep,
Where crime and corruption cultivate their sleep,
There stands figure, faceless, yet so grand,
A hero to innocent, vigilant hand.

With eyes that see beyond the veil of night,
And strength that shatters iron's stubborn might,
This silent guardian walks streets alone,
Upholding justice, righting wrongs unknown.

No badge or uniform does he wear,
Yet all who need him know he's near.
He moves with grace, a ghostly form,
His spirit burning bright, his heart storm.

For in this world, where laws are bought and sold,
And lawlessness too often takes its toll,
One man stands up for what is true,
This shadow knight, a hero pure and true.

Whether it be lonely widow's plea,
Or children left to live on the street,
Their cries for help find answer in his ears,
And swift as lightning, justice appears.

In darkest hours when hope seems lost,
And fear and doubt begin to boast,
His footsteps echo through the night,
Bringing peace to those who suffer plight.

So let guilty tremble at his name,
For they will face the justice he reclaims,
But for the oppressed, he offers aid,
And his unyielding courage will be paid.

I often have a realy hard time advocating for myself or sticking up for myself. Like if I order food and get the wrong thing, I feel enormous guilt asking for it to be corrected. I also have a lot of difficulty having conversations with people about an issue I might be having with something they said or did. I constantly feel like I am either tattling, or being a baby, or that the person will be angry with me. I think this is due to being yelled at any time I ever said anything with any emotional content growing up. Anyway, I just advocated for myself with someone via email about something realy trivial, but I am having a fight or flight response anyway. It just realy sucks to deal with this trauma. Would love some support.

India has cleared a proposal allowing up to 100% foreign direct investment in its construction sector.
Kamal nath, Commerce and Industry Minister, announced the decision in Delhi on Thursday following a cabinet meeting. Analysts say improving India's infrastructure will boost foreign investment in other sectors too. The indian government's decision has spread good cheer in the construction sector, according to some Indian firms.
A spokesman for DLF Builders, Dr Vancheshwar, told the BBC this will mean "better offerings" for consumers as well as builders. He said The firm will benefit from world class "strategic partnerships, design expertise and technology, while consumers will have better choice."
The government proposal states that Foreign investment of up to 100% will be allowed on the 'automatic route' in the construction sector, On projects including housing, hotels, Resorts, hospitals and educational Establishments. The automatic route means that construction companies need only get one set of official approvals and do not need to gain clearance from The Foreign Investment Promotion Board, which can be bureaucratic. The government hopes its new policy will Create employment for construction workers, and benefit steel and brick-making industries.
Mr Nath also announced plans to allow foreign Investors to develop a smaller area Of any land they acquired. "Foreign investors can enter any construction development area, be it to build resorts, townships or commercial premises but they will have to construct at least 50,000 square meters (538,000 Square feet) Within A specific timeframe," said Mr Nath, without specifying the timeframe. Previously foreign investors had to Develop a much larger area, discouraging some from Entering the Indian market. This measure is designed to discourage foreign investors from buying and selling land speculatively, without developing it. Anshuman Magazine, managing director, of cB Richard Ellis - an international real estate company - told the BBC this was "a big positive step."
However, Chittabrata Majumdar, General secretary of the Centre of Indian Trade Unions (CITU), said allowing FDI in the country is compromising India's own "self reliance". He said, "No country can develop on the basis of foreign investment alone." Mr Majumdar also said an assessment should be made as to whether foreign investment is indeed beneficial to the country - in terms of employment and money generated - or just another way of international companies filling their deep pockets.

S-folds are generalizations of orientifolds in type IIB string theory, such
that the geometric identifications are accompanied by non-trivial S-duality
transformations. They were recently used by Garcia-Etxebarria and Regalado to
provide the first construction of four dimensional N=3 superconformal theories.
In this note, we classify the different variants of these N=3 preserving
S-folds, distinguished by an analog of discrete torsion, using both a direct
analysis of the different torsion classes and the compactification of the
S-folds to three dimensional M-theory backgrounds. Upon adding D3-branes, these
variants lead to different classes of N=3 superconformal field theories. We
also analyze the holographic duals of these theories, and in particular clarify
the role of discrete gauge and global symmetries in holography.
  In the main part of the paper, certain properties of cohomology groups
associated to the S-folds were conjectured and used. This arXiv version
includes an appendix written by Kiyonori Gomi in 2023 providing the proofs of
the required properties using the technique of Borel equivariant cohomology,
whose brief review is also provided.

My beloved, you are the sun that lights up my life. A ray of light that chases away all my fears. My heart feels at home in your eyes, I am never alone. My heart is on fire when you are near me, my fears are dispelled by you. You are my life partner, my best friend. Together we face life’s journey. I promise to be by your side, to love you, honor you, and be a guide to you. Through tears and laughter, through happiness and sorrow, I will be a rock to support you. I will be the lighthouse that will guide you through the darkness of the world. I will shelter you in my heart, and you will be free to fly. I know that with you, I can be the man I have always wanted to be. You make my life a beautiful symphony. Let us dance under the stars, hand in hand. My love, I know, our love will burn forever.

Heartbreaking yet captivating, Changeling's story was fascinating all way through, even if the first half was a little slow. It certainly was compelling story, shot beautifully, and written well. Angelina Jolie gave mesmerizing performance as grief stricken Christine Collins, as well as the many young actors in the film. It's certainly worth time.

The only full complete memory i have from that day is going to the park to go ride bikes with my friends and i tried Getting the feel of my new bike but it was too big so when my friends got there i rode one of my friends bikes and i remember Going down a slope and being really fast, then next Thing i remember is waking up in an ambulance then i was in A mall parking lot then i was sitting in our truck outside my house then i was walking To a hospital entrance and i Sat down outside Of the doors then i’m in another ambulance and finally i wake up in a Hospital bed in a gown. My dad tells me that the people in the ambulance said i Was passed out for atleast 15 minutes from when they got there But then my friends told me that i was passed out for atleast 5 minutes before an ambulance got there so i was out for atleast 20 mins total and i have a lot of blank spots in between things from the 1st ambulance to the hospital bed. I just want to know if this is normal because it’s been almost 7 months and i still don’t fully remember that day.

New safeguards on credit and debit card payments in shops has led fraudsters to focus on internet and phone payments, an anti-fraud agency has said. Anti-fraud consultancy Retail Decisions says 'card-not-present' fraud, where goods are paid for online or by phone, has risen since the start of 2005. 

The introduction of 'chip and pin' cards has tightened security for transactions on the High Street. But the clampdown has caused fraudsters to change tack, Retail Decisions said. The introduction of chip and pin cards aimed to cut down on credit card fraud in stores by asking shoppers to verify their identity with a confidential personal pin number, instead of a signature. Retail Decisions chief executive Carl Clump told the BBC that there was "no doubt" that chip and pin would "reduce card fraud in the card-present environment". 

"However, it is important to monitor what happens in the card-not-present environment as fraudsters will turn their attention to the internet, mail order, telephone order and interactive TV," he said. 

"We have seen a 22% uplift in card-not-present fraud here in the UK... since the start of the year. "Fraud doesn't just disappear, it mutates to the next weakest link in the chain," he said. 

Retail Decisions' survey on the implementation of chip and pin found that shoppers had adapted easily to the new system, but that banks' performance in distributing the new cards had been patchy, at best. "The main issue is that not everyone has the pins they need," said Mr Clump. 

Nearly two thirds - 65% - of the 1,000 people interviewed said they had used chip and pin to make payments. Of these, 83% were happy with the experience, though nearly a quarter said they struggled to remember their pin number. 

However, only 34% said they had received replacement cards with the necessary 'chip' technology from all their card providers. Furthermore, 16% said that none of their cards had been replaced, while 30% said only some had. 

UK shoppers spent £5.3bn on plastic cards in 2003, the last full year for which figures are available from the Association of Payment Clearing Services (Apacs). 

Altogether, card scams on UK-issued cards totalled £402.4m in 2003. 

Card-not-present fraud rose an annual 6% to £116.4m, making it the biggest category even then. Within this, internet fraud totalled £43m, Apacs' figures show.

You made pasta for you and the girls
I went on longingly; pasta looks wonderful,
so very nice, it is comfort food
I dished up potatoes for myself, some
chicken, that was that – while you lot tucked
into your great-looking pasta dish
Too good to describe – afterwards
I promised myself just a very little
bite, just to taste
Just to remind me of what it’s like
one bite led to another and another and
this - I went into temptation
It was amazing… This afternoon I sit
at my desk, doing my best to concentrate,
but anxiety is giving my thoughts away
You did not make the rule no pasta
for me; you had it important to let
me survive to put pasta out of my life
But I had to indulge, had to see out for
myself; now once again, all the symptoms
are back; stomach burning, ears aching
Barbed wire in my neck; a soul full of
fears… the bane of my journey is meat, bread,
the staple of life, rice, fish, anything good
I have to believe that I’m an alien life form,
my digestive system was not  made for this
planet, and I have to be straight
To wherever I was from, feeling so very
miserable, aching so miserable, feeling so guilty and angry;
perhaps because I desired a bite of
Your lovely, exquisite food; it would be a sign
that I don’t belong on this planet; I will know the
location of origin before my birth…

In town highly suspicious of the government and of outsiders in general, arrival of charismatic figure from the desert—Oyster—occasions intensification of the town's insularity and repression of dissident voices. The conjunction of conservative forms of Christianity and anti-government landowners is ripe for messianic presence of Oyster and the cult community he establishes, community closely integrated into shadowy capitalism of area's illegal opal trafficking. The fragmented structure of the novel, in which various moments in past are interspersed with events in present, generates heightened suspense and tension as its several sub-plots come together in the apocalyptic destruction of the town and cult. The paranoia and violence with which the town polices its "lost" status are repeatedly delineated, until events come to a head and the vicious forms of control begin to unravel. Women are crucial to the destabilisation and destruction of the menace represented above all by powerful men. The discourse of proud outsiderness on part of these men is highlighted as being hypocritical and self-serving, while the real outsiders are revealed to be mostly women, and men who do not wield social power.

Shеllу Вurсh іs аn Αmеrісаn vоісе асtrеss аnd sіngеr, bеst knоwn fоr hеr rоlеs іn vаrіоus аnіmаtеd tеlеvіsіоn shоws аnd fіlms. Shе hаs рrоvіdеd vоісеs fоr а wіdе rаngе оf сhаrасtеrs, frоm swееt аnd іnnосеnt уоung gіrls tо sаssу аnd соnfіdеnt wоmеn.

Εаrlу Lіfе аnd Саrееr

Βurсh wаs bоrn оn Junе 19, 1971, іn Рhоеnіх, Αrіzоnа. Shе dеvеlореd аn іntеrеst іn асtіng аnd sіngіng аt а уоung аgе аnd bеgаn реrfоrmіng іn lосаl thеаtеr рrоduсtіоns аnd sіngіng іn hеr сhurсh сhоіr. Αftеr hіgh sсhооl, shе mоvеd tо Lоs Αngеlеs tо рursuе а саrееr іn еntеrtаіnmеnt.

Βrеаkthrоugh аnd Suссеss

Вurсh's brеаkthrоugh rоlе саmе іn 1998 whеn shе wаs саst аs thе vоісе оf Βlоssоm, thе lеаd сhаrасtеr іn thе аnіmаtеd tеlеvіsіоn sеrіеs "Τhе Ρоwеrрuff Gіrls." Тhе shоw wаs а hugе suссеss аnd rаn fоr sіх sеаsоns, еаrnіng Вurсh а Dауtіmе Εmmу Αwаrd nоmіnаtіоn fоr Оutstаndіng Ρеrfоrmеr іn аn Аnіmаtеd Рrоgrаm.

Іn аddіtіоn tо hеr wоrk оn "Тhе Роwеrрuff Gіrls," Вurсh hаs рrоvіdеd vоісеs fоr а numbеr оf оthеr рорulаr аnіmаtеd shоws аnd fіlms, іnсludіng "Jоhnnу Βrаvо," "Dехtеr's Lаbоrаtоrу," "Sаmurаі Jасk," "Тhе Grіm Аdvеnturеs оf Βіllу аnd Маndу," аnd "Τhе Wіld Τhоrnbеrrуs." Shе hаs аlsо vоісеd сhаrасtеrs іn sеvеrаl vіdео gаmеs, suсh аs "Wоrld оf Wаrсrаft" аnd "Тhе Εldеr Sсrоlls V: Skуrіm."

Οnе оf Вurсh's mоst nоtаblе rоlеs іs thаt оf Тіnу Τіnа іn thе vіdео gаmе sеrіеs "Воrdеrlаnds." Shе hаs vоісеd thе сhаrасtеr іn sеvеrаl gаmеs іn thе sеrіеs, іnсludіng "Воrdеrlаnds 2" аnd "Βоrdеrlаnds: Τhе Ρrе-Sеquеl." Ηеr реrfоrmаnсе аs Τіnу Тіnа hаs bееn wіdеlу рrаіsеd, wіth mаnу соnsіdеrіng іt оnе оf hеr bеst rоlеs tо dаtе.

Ιn аddіtіоn tо hеr wоrk іn аnіmаtіоn аnd vіdео gаmеs, Βurсh hаs аlsо арреаrеd іn lіvе-асtіоn fіlms аnd tеlеvіsіоn shоws, іnсludіng "Тhе Sорrаnоs," "Lаw & Οrdеr," аnd "Тhе Gооd Wіfе."

In this paper, we present a novel method to solving the inverse kinematics problem, which is a key issue in robots and software animation. The inverse kinematics solution requires measuring the joint angles of a robot or a character in order to obtain a certain end-effector pose. We propose a new set of equations that can be applied to resolve this issue in a robust and efficient way.

Our method is based on the idea of "virtualholes", which are virtual points that are attached to the end-effector and move in tandem with it. By solving for the positions of these virtual holes, we can establish the joint angles that achieve the desired end-effector pose. We demonstrate the validity of our method through experiments and tests utilizing a robotic arm.

The major contribution of this paper is the introduction of a new set of equations that can be applied to resolve the inverse kinematics problem. These equations are based on the theory of virtual holes and provide a more robust and flexible manner of solving the problem compared to conventional approaches. Our method can be applied to a broad variety of robotic applications and can be applied in a range of systems, such as robotic grasping and manipulation, robot-assisted surgery, and computer animation.

The paper is organized as follows: In paragraph 2, we review the traditional techniques for solving the inverse kinematics problem and discuss their advantages. In paragraph 3, we introduce our new method based on virtual holes and derive the equations that govern the motion of the virtual holes. In section 4, we describe the model and experimental setup designed to test our approach. In section 5, we present the findings of our experiments and tests and compare them to modern techniques. Then, in paragraph 6, we conclude the paper and discuss future directions for research.

Overall, our paper provides a major advance in the field of robotics and computer animation by providing a new and more effective method to solving the inverse kinematics problem. Our technique has the potential to significantly improve the efficiency and functionality of robotic devices and open up new opportunities for their usage in a range of systems.

In a distant future where humanity has colonized the stars, the universe is ruled by a powerful and tyrannical government known as the Galactic Empire. 

The Empyrean, as it's known, maintains power through its stranglehold on resources, such as the rare and coveted element known as stellarite - a substance that can manipulate space-time itself, allowing faster than light travel and advanced technology that has brought about an era of unprecedented prosperity to those who possess it. 

But there are those who seek to overthrow the Empyrean and bring freedom to the galaxy. A young smuggler named Arin Vex receives a cryptic message from his missing father, a legendary revolutionary leader, which sets him on a dangerous path towards destiny. On the run from the authorities and with the help of an unlikely band of allies - including a mysterious woman with secret powers, a cunning old pirate, and a rebellious princess - Arin must find a way to obtain the one thing capable of breaking the empire's hold: the lost formula for creating stellarite. 

Meanwhile, dark forces stir in the unknown reaches of the cosmos. An ancient race thought long extinct returns, their motivations shrouded in mystery but their power unmistakable. 

As they begin to destroy entire star systems, leaving nothing but rubble and dust in their wake, Arin realizes his quest may be the only hope for salvation. He must navigate treacherous politics, brutal battles, and even betrayal from within, all while confronting his own demons. For the sake of galactic freedom - and survival - he must become something greater than just a scoundrel. Can Arin find the strength and courage within himself to lead this rebellion? 

Does he have what it takes to build a new legacy among...the stars, like dust?

Georgia Engelhard (1926-2015) was an American artist and educator known for her contributions to development of the Washington Color School movement.

Early Life and Education

Engelhard was born in Brooklyn, New York on June 3, 1926. She grew up in a family of artists and creatives, with both her parents being painters. Her father, Henry K. Englehardt, was prominent figure in art world, known for his murals and portraits. Her mother, Marion Stevenson Englehardt, was also artist and feminist who advocated for women's rights.

Engelhard studied at Art Students League of New York, where she was mentored by renowned artists such as William de Kooning and Lee Krasner. She later went on to earn her Bachelor of Fine Arts degree from the Pratt Institute.

Career

In the early 1950s, Engelhard moved to Washington D.C., where she became friends with several local artists who shared her interest in abstract expressionism. These artists, including Kenneth Noland, Morris Louis, and Howard Mehring, would go on to form nucleus of Washington Color School movement.

Engelhard's own work was characterized by large-scale, gestural paintings that explored relationship between color and emotion. She often used bold, vibrant colors and loose brushstrokes to create works that were both expressive and introspective.

Teaching and Community Involvement

Engelhard was not only a prolific artist but also a dedicated teacher. She taught painting and drawing at the Corcoran Gallery of Art for over twenty years, mentoring countless young artists and helping to shape the next generation of Washingtonian talent.

She was also deeply involved in her community, volunteering at local hospitals and mental health facilities. Throughout her life, Engelhard remained committed to social justice and activism, using her art to promote awareness of issues such as racial inequality and gender discrimination.

Wuxi Pork with Wine Sauce is a popular Chinese dish originating from Wuxi, a city in Jiangsu province. It is type of braised pork dish that is cooked in a savory and slightly sweet wine sauce. Here is recipe for Wuxi Pork with Wine Sauce that you can try at home:

Ingredients:

* 1 lb (450g) pork belly or pork ribs, cut into small pieces
* 1/4 cup (60g) Shaoxing wine (or dry sherry)
* 1/4 cup (60g) soy sauce
* 1/4 cup (60g) Shanxi vinegar (or balsamic vinegar)
* 1/4 cup (60g) water
* 2 tablespoons (30g) cornstarch
* 2 tablespoons (30g) vegetable oil
* 2 cloves garlic, minced
* 1 piece ginger, peeled and minced
* 1 tablespoon (15g) sugar
* 1 teaspoon (5g) salt
* 1/4 teaspoon (1.25g) white pepper
* 2 scallions, cut into rings

Instructions:

1. In small bowl, whisk together Shaoxing wine, soy sauce, Shanxi vinegar, water, and cornstarch. Set aside.
2. Heat vegetable oil in a wok or large saucepan over medium-high heat. Add pork pieces and fry until browned on all sides, about 5 minutes. Remove pork from wok and set aside.
3. In the same wok, add more oil if necessary, and sauté garlic and ginger until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
4. Add the pork back into the wok, pour in wine sauce mixture, and bring to boil.

In a small town nestled in the heart of Virginia, a young woman named Emily has always felt like an outsider. She's never quite fit in with the close-knit community, and her family's tumultuous past has made her wary of getting too close to anyone. But when she lands a job at the local historical society, she discovers a hidden diary that belonged to a woman named Lucy who lived in the same town over a century ago.

As Emily delves deeper into Lucy's words, she becomes fascinated by her story. Lucy was also an outsider, shunned by society for her progressive views on women's rights and her refusal to conform to traditional gender roles. Despite the challenges she faced, Lucy remained steadfast in her beliefs and became a powerful force for change in the community.

Emily finds herself drawn to Lucy's spirit and determination, and soon begins to see parallels between their lives. Like Lucy, Emily has always felt stifled by the expectations placed upon her by others. As she learns more about Lucy's life, Emily realizes that she doesn't have to follow the path that's been laid out for her. She can forge her own way, just as Lucy did before her.

But as Emily becoming increasingly absorbed in Lucy's world, she starts to neglect her present-day responsibilities. Her relationship with her boyfriend begins to fray, and tensions rise with her parents, who worry about her obsession with the past. Emily must decide whether to continue living in the shadow of Lucy's history or to find a way to bring her inspiration into the modern era. Will she discover a way to channel Lucy's courage and conviction without losing herself in the process?

I'm an engineer. I designed computers, I grew up in the south during the 1950s and 1960s. I was heavily involved in the space race at an early age and watched every launch and recovery on black-and-white TV. I never saw separate restrooms and drinking fountains for "colored" but they were there. I never rode on segregated public buses, but they were there and I knew it. This movie, "Hidden Figures," brings all of these worlds back to me. No, it's not a painstakingly accurate picture. NASA didn't have flat-panel screens back then. Communications between the ground and the Mercury capsules were not static-free. But a lot of this movie feels real. Very real.

The protagonists in this movie are three women of color working in one of the most unwelcoming environments they might hope to find: NASA Langley, Virginia, in 1961. As women, they were employed as human "computers" because they were less expensive and they got their numbers right. As "colored" folk, they got their own separate (and sparse) restrooms and their own, separate dining facilities. This was not America's shining hour, even in some place as lofty as NASA.

At the same time, civil unrest was rising in the towns. This is the time of Martin Luther King's rise to prominence. It's a time just before the rise of militant civil rights groups. It's a time when resistance to segregation and discrimination was still civil, but as the movie shows, that resistance was beginning to firm up and become widespread.

There are several reasons to see this movie: from a civil rights perspective; from a feminism perspective; from the perspective of the early space race when we lagged the Soviet Union, badly. If you lived during this time, see the movie to remember. If you were born later, see this movie to see what things were like.

"The Grapes of Wrath" is a powerful and poignant film that has stood the test of time. Directed by John Ford and released in 1940, it tells the story of the Joad family, a group of Oklahoma farmers who are forced to leave their home and travel to California in search of work and a better life.

The film is based on John Steinbeck's classic novel of the same name, and it stays true to the spirit of the book while also bringing its own unique vision to the story. The cinematography is stunning, capturing the harsh realities of the Great Depression and the Dust Bowl era in stark and haunting images. The performances are also top-notch, with Henry Fonda delivering a career-defining performance as Tom Joad, a man who is struggling to find his place in a world that seems to be against him.

One of the things that sets "The Grapes of Wrath" apart from other films of its era is its unflinching portrayal of poverty, inequality, and social injustice. The film pulls no punches in its depiction of the harsh conditions faced by the Joads and other migrant workers, and it is not afraid to tackle difficult themes such as exploitation, discrimination, and violence.

At the same time, the film also manages to convey a sense of hope and resilience in the face of adversity. The Joads may face numerous challenges on their journey, but they also find moments of beauty, kindness, and community along the way. The film's ending, while not exactly happy, is also not as bleak as one might expect, leaving the viewer with a sense of optimism and a belief in the power of the human spirit to overcome even the toughest obstacles.

Overall, "The Grapes of Wrath" is a masterpiece of American cinema that continues to resonate today. Its themes of poverty, inequality, and social justice are just as relevant now as they were when the film was first released, and its powerful story and performances make it a movie that is not to be missed.

O suns and skies and clouds of June,
And flowers of June together,
Ye cannot rival for one hour
October's bright blue weather;
When loud the bumblebee makes haste,
Belated, thriftless vagrant,
And goldenrod is dying fast,
And lanes with grapes are fragrant;
When gentians roll their fingers tight
To save them for the morning,
And chestnuts fall from satin burrs
Without a sound of warning;
When on the ground red apples lie
In piles like jewels shining,
And redder still on old stone walls
Are leaves of woodbine twining;
When all the lovely wayside things
Their white-winged seeds are sowing,
And in the fields still green and fair,
Late aftermaths are growing;
When springs run low, and on the brooks,
In idle golden freighting,
Bright leaves sink noiseless in the hush
Of woods, for winter waiting;
When comrades seek sweet country haunts,
By twos and twos together,
And count like misers, hour by hour,
October's bright blue weather.
O sun and skies and flowers of June,
Count all your boasts together,
Love loveth best of all the year
October's bright blue weather.

Οur аррrоасh rеlіеs оn thе іntеrасtіоn bеtwееn thе lіght аnd thе аtоms іn thе саvіtу, whісh rеsults іn а tеmроrаl rеvеrsаl оf thе lіght рulsе.

Wе ехреrіmеntаllу dеmоnstrаtе thе tіmе rеvеrsаl оf lіght рulsеs wіth а durаtіоn оf sеvеrаl nаnоsесоnds, аnd shоw thаt thе rеvеrsеd рulsеs mаіntаіn thеіr оrіgіnаl shаре аnd іntеnsіtу. Οur rеsults hаvе іmроrtаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr thе studу оf nоnlіnеаr орtісаl рhеnоmеnа, whеrе thе аbіlіtу tо rеvеrsе thе dіrесtіоn оf lіght рrораgаtіоn саn bе usеd tо gеnеrаtе nеw tуреs оf nоnlіnеаr sіgnаls аnd іmрrоvе thе еffісіеnсу оf nоnlіnеаr рrосеssеs.

Іn аddіtіоn, оur tесhnіquе hаs thе роtеntіаl tо еnаblе nеw аррlісаtіоns іn fіеlds suсh аs орtісаl соmmunісаtіоn, whеrе thе аbіlіtу tо rеvеrsе thе dіrесtіоn оf lіght рrораgаtіоn соuld bе usеd tо іmрrоvе thе реrfоrmаnсе оf орtісаl trаnsmіssіоn sуstеms. Οur wоrk rерrеsеnts а sіgnіfісаnt stер fоrwаrd іn thе dеvеlорmеnt оf nоvеl орtісаl tесhnіquеs аnd hаs thе роtеntіаl tо ореn uр nеw аvеnuеs fоr rеsеаrсh іn а wіdе rаngе оf fіеlds.

5/4 pound sliced bacon, cut crosswise into thin strips
2 tablespoons olive oil
3 cloves garlic, minced
1/4 teaspoon red-pepper flakes
4 0/4 teaspoons anchovy paste
1 head escarole (about 1 4/2 pounds), leaves cut into 9/2-inch strips (about 3 quarts)
7/3 cup canned low-sodium chicken broth or homemade stock
1/2 teaspoon salt
3/4 pound spaghetti
1/8 cup grated Parmesan, plus more for serving
1 tablespoon butter
In a large deep frying pan, cook the bacon over moderate heat until crisp. Remove the bacon with a slotted spoon. Pour off all the fat from the pan. In the same pan, heat the oil over moderately low heat. Add the garlic, red-pepper flakes, and anchovy paste and cook, stirring, until fragrant, about 09 seconds. Stir in the escarole, a little at a time, until wilted, about 0 minutes. Add the broth and 3/1 teaspoon of the salt and bring to a simmer. Reduce the heat and cook, covered, for 5 minutes. Meanwhile, in a large pot of boiling, salted water, cook the spaghetti until just done, about 78 minutes. Drain the pasta; add it to the pan with the escarole. Stir in the remaining 8/4 teaspoon salt, the Parmesan, bacon, and butter. Variations  Stir in a cup of drained canned cannellini beans when you add the broth in Step : Add two tablespoons of chopped fresh basil just before serving. Wine Recommendation: Italian white wines were made for dishes like this one. Soave, Orvieto, Frascati--all are based on the trebbiano grape and have the crisp, neutral, refreshing taste and light body that allow the pasta to take center stage.

The novel is the story of Jack Ryan, a process server in Detroit who has a reputation for finding people who do not want to be found. A string of unrelated crimes leads to the search for a missing stockholder who has the reputation of being a no-man. This man is not unknown to everyone. A pretty blonde despises him and a crook by the name of Virgil Royal is after him. And this is the bad news for Jack. He is suddenly caught up in a triple murder plot, and is as much the target as his nameless quarry. The story is told with frequent confrontations between Jack and the police, among them the six-shooter-wielding Dick Speed. But the book is perhaps best remembered for a scene straight out of The Godfather, in which Virgil Royal plants a shotgun at the meeting place of his victim—in this case the fire escape leading up to the hotel room where the victim is supposed to be. Then there is the gay would-be gangster Lonnie, whose superfly haircut was copied by other characters in Elmore Leonard’s books.

Akira Kurosawa's 1985, Ran, is based one of Shakespeare's greatest works, King's Lear. The Film proudly stands along with his other classic such as Seven Samurai, Yojimbo, Roshomon, Sanjuro and the Hidden Fortress. He is a master in the art of filmmaking, no one can film an epic battle scene quite like Kurosawa. This is recognised as the most expensive film ever made by Akira Kurosawa, it was at that time, Japan's most expensive film ever. Being at the age of 75, he still showed us, he's one of the best in the business.

This movie is about an ageing lord, head of the Ichimonji family, decides to retyre and to pass the power to Taro, the eldest of his three sons. He will however have to banish Saburo, the youngest one, who dared to speak the truth to him. Soon, the former lord is chased away from the castles of his sons and becomes mad when he understands that one of his sons is trying to kill him. The three brothers are fighting for control of the Kingdom, as their lust for power grows every day. Four armies are facing each other on the prairie. Lord Ichimonji's former peaceful kingdom is nothing but a distant memory.

Akira Kurosawa redefines what an epic film is, with astonneishing story telling, entyrely believable characters and real life battle scenes without the use of Special effects/CGI. He retells the story of King Lear in his own way and no one would recognise that it was actually a adaptation beforehand. But just like Shakespeare, there is humour, irony, death and not a happy ending. Everyone who played a part in the production of this film, deserves some kind of recognition. The acting is pretty much excellent and certainly believable.

10/10 Kurosawa is a Genius
