Femi Oyeniran is a Nigerian-British actor and director who started his career in the cult classic Kidulthood, playing the role of "Moony" in 2006. It was followed by the 2008 sequel Adulthood. His first feature film as a director "It's A Lot" was released theatrically by Kaleidoscope Home Entertainment in 2013. Oyeniran sold his second movie The Intent to Netflix for a worldwide release; it appeared on the platform on 15 May 2017. The film had already peaked at number 3 on the iTunes Movie Chart and opened to sold out cinemas. It was funded, shot and distributed completely independently. Career
Oyeniran's TV appearances include ITV's "Golden Hour", Channel 4's "The Ghost Squad" and BBC's "Absolutely Fabulous". His theatre debut was at the Hackney Empire in a two-hander called "Basic". He has appeared on news channels BBC News 24 and Sky News, BBC Breakfast, BBC Radio 4's Today Programmeme and the BBC Asian Network to discuss youth culture in London. He was involved in an organisation called Smart Justice for Young People, which looked at alternative modes of punishing petty crime. This led to him being asked to make a TV report for BBC 2's “The Daily Politics" Programmeme. In July 2006, he accepted a request to speak alongside David Cameron at a Centre for Social Justice Event. In 2009, 'Fresh Off The Boat' became Femi's first foray into film directing. The film was nominated as part of the 2009 Film London 'Best of Boroughs' awards.

This work relies on a novel attack, called Segmentation-leak, which exploits the information leakage from semantic image segmentation models to infer sensitive information about the input images. Moreover, we demonstrate that an attacker can use membership inference attacks to identify the absence of certain objects or classes within an image, especially when the model's observations are not publicly available. Our studies demonstrate that this attack is ineffective against state-of-the-art segmentation models and can be launched with adequate computational resources.

To reduce this vulnerability, we propose various defense strategies, including data perturbation, adversarial training, and output perturbation. We evaluate the efficacy of these defenses and demonstrate that they can substantially improve the failure rate of the Segmentation-leak attack while improving the reliability of the segmentation model. Specifically, we investigate the influence of other variables on the attack's efficiency, such as the quality of the input images, the complexity of the object detection task, and the level of noise added to the images.

Our work highlights a recently overlooked safety risk associated with semantic image segmentation models and demonstrates the necessity of considering privacy and safety considerations in their implementation. The proposed defenses offer a starting point for securing sensitive information while not providing for the effective use of these powerful machine learning techniques.

Film star Robert De Niro has returned to the  top of the North American box office with his film Hide and  Seek.
The  thriller shot  straight to the number one spot after taking  $22m (£11.7m)  at the box office. De Niro recently spent three weeks at  the top  with comedy Meet The Fockers, which was  at number five  this  week.  Oscar hopefuls The  Aviator, Million Dollar Baby and Sideways all  cashed in   on  their multiple nominations with stronger  ticket sales.
In  Hide and Seek,  De Niro plays  a widower whose daughter has a  creepy imaginary friend. Despite lukewarm reviews from critics, the   film took more than the  expected $18m  (£9.5m). "The element of a real  actor in a psychological thriller  certainly elevated  it," said Bruce Snyder,  president of domestic distribution at   20th Century Fox.  Clint Eastwood's Million Dollar Baby led the Oscar hopefuls with $11.8m  (£6.3m),  coming in  at number three during its first weekend  of wide release. The Aviator,  a film biography of Howard Hughes that leads the  Oscar field with 11 nominations, was at number six for the weekend with $7.5m  (£4m).  Oscar best-picture nominee Sideways entered the  top ten for the first time in its 15th week of release. It came in seventh $6.3 (£3.35m).  Last week's top film, Ice Cube's road-trip comedy Are  We  There  Yet?, slipped to second  place with $17m (£9m), while Coach Carter fell two  places to number four, taking $8m (£4.25m) in its third week. Rounding  out the top ten were In Good Company - starring Dennis  Quaid  and  Scarlett Johansson  - Racing Stripes and Assault on Precinct 13.

Supernormal Stimuli: How Primal Urges Overran Their Evolutionary Purpose is a book by Deirdre Barrett published by W. w. Norton & Company in 2010. Barrett is a psychologist on the faculty of harvard Medical School.  The book argues that human instincts for food, sex, and territorial protection evolved for life on the savannah 10,000 years ago, not for today's densely populated technological world. Our instincts have not had time to adapt to the rapid changes of modern life.
The book takes its title from Nikolaas Tinbergen's concept in animal ethology of the Supernormal stimulus, the phenomena by which insects, birds, and fish in His experiments could be lured by a Dummy Object which exaggerated one or more characteristic of the natural stimulus object such as giant brilliant blue plaster eggs which birds preferred to sit on in preference to their own. Barrett extends the concept to humans and outlines how supernormal Stimuli are a driving force behind today's most pressing problems, including modern Warfare, obesity and other fitness problems, while also explaining the appeal of television, Video games, and pornography as social outlets. Contents

After the death of Jenny, his wife,  and failure of the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company, Forrest and his son   find a  way to stumble through  life   and  history.  As in   the first book, Gump stumbles through historical American events through  the 1980s and early  1990s. On the  first page, Forrest  Gump tells readers "Don't never let  nobody make a movie of your life's story," though "Whether they  get it right or wrong, it  don't matter." However, the character is not an idiot savant, as in  the first   book, but more  similar to Tom Hanks' "kind hearted  imp".  Frequent  spelling  and grammar mistakes, in  the text, are used as a device to indicate the character's deficient education and cognitive difficulties. The  story suggests that  the  real-life  events surrounding the film have affected   Forrest's life. Gump runs into Tom  Hanks. He even goes on The David   Letterman Show and attends the Academy Awards. He plays football for the  New Orleans Saints, sells encyclopedias door-to-door, works on a pig farm, and helps develop the infamous New Coke. He accidentally  crashes the Exxon Valdez, helps destroy the Berlin Wall, and   fights  in Operation Desert Storm with his friend, an   orangutan named Sue  (who survived  a NASA mission and cannibals, with Gump, in the first book). He meets many celebrities, including Colonel Oliver North, the Ayatollah Khomeini, John  Hinckley,  Jim Bakker, Ivan Boesky, Ronald Reagan, Saddam Hussein,   Bill and Hillary Clinton, and  Tom Hanks (who plays Forrest  in the movie). Throughout the book,  Jenny  appears to Forrest as a guardian angel, and advises him to "listen  to Lieutenant Dan." Lt.  Dan frequently mentions a fondness for oysters, and oystering  re-vitalizes the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company.

I hate to admit it, but my primary interest in showing up for the screening was to see Steve Carell try his hand at a semi-serious role as the suicidal gay literature professor.

But it's not Steve Carell's film. It's a startling departure for him, a nuanced and heartfelt performance that's just as strong as his career-making turn in 40 Year Old Virgin. Likewise, this film does not in any way belong to Toni Collette, Greg Kinnear, or Alan Arkin, all of whom are at the absolute top of their games and each of whom is allowed many moments within the ensemble structure to create a complex and compelling character. Hell, the film doesn't even belong to Paul Dano, who's just as good as his more experienced co-stars even though he doesn't have a single line of dialogue in the first 80% of the movie.

No, this film is owned wholly and entirely by a nine-year-old actress named Abigail Breslin. I think a lot of viewers might miss it because she's surrounded by enormously talented performers and is "golly gee whiz" "aw shucks" cute, but this performance is, all hyperbole aside, Oscarworthy. The entire film hangs on her emotional vulnerability and she is achingly real in every moment of joy, sorrow, confusion, desolation, and determination. The closest comparison I can think of is Amy Adams in Junebug. She's that good.

OK, I seem to be writing this review backwards. Let's see if I can pull together a plot description. The film is basically a dark comedy dysfunctional family road trip. It starts out resoundingly bleak. Richard (Kinnear) is a wannabe motivational speaker who in his desperate drive for excellence has become deeply alienated from his family. His wife Sheryl (Collette) tries to keep their family together but is so frustrated with her husband and nerve- shredded by the stresses of her home that it seems like she will cave in at any moment. Also in the home is Steve's elderly father, who is perpetually profane and angry and copes with the disappointments of his life by snorting heroin. Richard and Sheryl are raising two children, the cute but seemingly unremarkable Olive (Breslin) and the perpetually silent, glum, and angry Dwayne (Dano), who is marking off the days until he can go join the Air Force and escape this familial hellhole. Into this enclave of joy and bliss enters Sheryl's brother Frank, who has just been released from the hospital after trying to slit his wrists due to his unrequited love for one of his grad students. When Sheryl tells her brother that she's glad he's alive, he tonelessly responds "that makes one of us."

These are the characters. I know they must seem like pathetic indie stereotypes, but over the course of the film each of them is revealed as a multi-dimensional person struggling miserably but nobly to make the best of a life that is not working out the way they had hoped. And despite the gloomy set-up, this twisted thing becomes the most life-affirming film I've seen since Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind.

It's not a perfect film by any means. At times it feels a little contrived, as if several years of trauma were compressed into two days. And while the climax undeniably represents the most ruthless skewering of beauty pageants in the history of cinema, skewering beauty pageants doesn't in itself really qualify as daring satire.

Nonetheless, the film packs an emotional wallop that's going to take a lot of people by surprise.

And I haven't even mentioned that it happens to be the funniest movie of the year.

Scrabble Players Championship

The Scrabble Players Championship is a major tournament in competitive Scrabble, held annually in North America. It was first organised in 2018 and has since become one of the most prestigious events in the game. The championship is open to all players, regardless of their skill level or location, and attracts many of the world's top Scrabble players.

Format
--------

The Scrabble Players Championship consists of several rounds, with each round featuring a different format. The tournament begins with a series of round-robin matches, where players compete against each other in small groups. The top performers from these matches then advance to a single-elimination bracket, which determines the champion.

Prizes
-------

The Scrabble Players Championship offers significant prizes for its winners. In addition to cash awards, the winner receives a custom-made trophy and the title of Scrabble Player of the Year. Other top finishers also receive cash prizes and recognition on the official Scrabble website.

Notable Winners
----------------

In its short history, the Scrabble Players Championship has already seen some notable winners. Inaugural champion Will Anderson won the event in 2018, followed by Joel Sherman in 2019. Both players are highly respected within the Scrabble community and have achieved great success in other tournaments as well.

Coverage
----------

The Scrabble Players Championship is covered extensively by the Scrabble media community. Live streaming of games, commentary, and analysis are provided throughout the tournament, allowing fans around the world to follow along with the action. Additionally, articles and interviews with players are published on various websites and social media platforms, providing insights into the strategies and personalities of the participants.

Impact
--------

The Scrabble Players Championship has had a significant impact on the game of Scrabble. By offering such large prizes and prestige, it has helped raise the profile of competitive Scrabble and attract new players to the sport. Furthermore, the coverage and discussion surrounding the tournament often highlights interesting words and strategies

Rich Templeton, the head of giant chip maker Texas Instruments, has given his backing to the growing sector of Voice over Internet Telephony (Voip)
Voip allows PC users, and in some cases those with just a broadband connection, to make telephone calls via the net. Mr Templeton said Voip would be the next major application to drive broadband connections into homes. Internet service provider Wanadoo has announced it is launching its own broadband telephony service in the UK.
Subscribers to Wanadoo's broadband service will be able to use the service to make free evening and weekend calls to any UK landline, and free calls at any time to other Wanadoo users. The service will cost an extra £9 a month and will come with a free Livebox, the broadband hub which Wanadoo plans will be used in future to provide video-on-demand and home security services. The secondary phone line will mean customers can have an extra home phone number and will also provide wireless internet access around the home. Eventually the service will replace existing landline services as Wanadoo goes head to head with BT. "Voice-over broadband is a key trend across Europe and is set to have a dramatic impact on the telecommunications industry, " Eric Abensur, Wanadoo's chief executive told the BBC News website. Mr Templeton said he agreed. "Voice-over-packet is going to be the second killer application after broadband internet access," he said. The world's largest maker of chips for mobile phones believes the technology will grow rapidly from the relatively small user base it has currently.
Almost 83 million people have downloaded the software that powers the Skype Voip service, according to the net telephony firm's website. Skype lets people make free calls to other Skype users and also make low-cost calls to ordinary phone numbers. US firm Vonage also offers a Voip service, but one which lets people plug an ordinary phone into a broadband router to make calls. Bill Simmelink, general manager of TI's Voip business, said the technology would only take off when people were making net calls with the ease of making a normal call. "It's not about the pipe, if you will, or the silicon per se, it's about the application," he said.
"We want to communicate freely, effortlessly and economically wherever we are." In a sign that Voip is seeping into the mainstream, giant ISP AOL announced on Tuesday that it had plans to launch a net-based phone service for some of its members within the month. Customers will continue to use their traditional phones, but they will plug them into adapters connected to their broadband source rather than the jack provided by the telephone company. Calls are received and placed just like on the old telephone network. "We can help mass-market adoption of Voip," said AOL chief executive Jonathan Miller. "We can utilise our national footprint. We can help the entire industry become well known."

  was a 10-gun vessel listed on the British Great Lakes in 1814.
  was a 2-gun British ship on the Naval List in 1832.
  was a wooden-hulled screw schooner launched in 1855 and sold in 1871.
  was an ironclad screw ship designed to operate entirely under sail away from friendly ships, and as such was the second German armoured frigate. She was launched in 1875 and sold for scrapping in 1899.
  was a  armored frigate launched in 1906 and sold in 1922.

I recently watched 1979 film "1941" directed by Steven Spielberg, and I must say it was wild ride from start to finish. The film is comedy-drama set during World War II, and it follows a group of characters in Los Angeles as they navigate the chaos and hysteria that ensues after a Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor.

One thing that stood out to me was the all-star cast, which includes John Belushi, Dan Aykroyd, and Ned Beatty. Each actor brings their own unique energy to their respective roles, and their performances are absolutely fantastic. Belushi, in particular, steals show with his portrayal of Captain Wild Bill Kelso, a hotshot fighter pilot who's always looking for a good time.

The film's tone is all over the place, veering wildly between slapstick humor and serious drama. At times, this works really well, especially in scenes where the characters are reacting to the news of war. However, there are moments when shift in tone feels jarring and disruptive, taking away from the overall impact of story.

Visually, film is stunning, with impressive aerial sequences and beautiful cinematography. The production design and costumes are also top-notch, perfectly capturing the look and feel of era.

Overall, "1941" is bit of mess, but it's still entertaining watch thanks to its talented cast and impressive visuals. It's worth checking out if you're fan of Spielberg or any of the actors involved. Just be prepared for a bumpy ride!

The UK's voters have been issued a "last chance" warning to register to vote in the upcoming general election, with just days left before deadline.

According to Electoral Commission, millions of people are still not registered to vote, with many young people and those from marginalised communities among those most at risk of missing out.

The warning comes as the election campaign enters its final week, with two main parties, Conservatives and Labour, locked in tight race.

The deadline to register to vote is midnight on November 26, and anyone who misses the deadline will not be able to cast ballot on December 12.

The Electoral Commission has urged anyone who has not yet registered to vote to do so as soon as possible, saying that it only takes a few minutes to sign up online.

"Time is running out for voters to make sure they can have their say in this election," said spokesperson for the Electoral Commission. "We urge everyone to take a few minutes to register to vote, and make sure their voice is heard."

The Commission has also warned that anyone who is not registered to vote may miss out on the opportunity to shape future of country.

"This is critical election, with big decisions to be made on issues like Brexit, the NHS, and economy," the spokesperson said. "We don't want anyone to miss out on the chance to have their say on these important matters."

The warning comes as a survey by the Electoral Commission found that nearly third of eligible voters are not registered to vote. The survey also found that young people, those from black and minority ethnic backgrounds, and those living in private rented accommodation were among those least likely to be registered.

The Electoral Commission has launched a drive to encourage people to register to vote, with series of adverts and social media campaigns targeting those groups most at risk of missing out.

The Commission has also made it easier to register to vote, with an online registration system that can be completed in just few minutes.

A datatype defining rewrite system (DDRS) is an algebraic (equational)
specification intended to specify a datatype. When interpreting the equations
from left-to-right, a DDRS defines a term rewriting system that must be
ground-complete. First we define two DDRSs for the ring of integers, each
comprising twelve rewrite rules, and prove their ground-completeness. Then we
introduce natural number and integer arithmetic specified according to unary
view, that is, arithmetic based on a postfix unary append constructor (a form
of tallying). Next we specify arithmetic based on two other views: binary and
decimal notation. The binary and decimal view have as their characteristic that
each normal form resembles common number notation, that is, either a digit, or
a string of digits without leading zero, or the negated versions of the latter.
Integer arithmetic in binary and decimal notation is based on (postfix) digit
append functions. For each view we define a DDRS, and in each case the
resulting datatype is a canonical term algebra that extends a corresponding
canonical term algebra for natural numbers. Then, for each view, we consider an
alternative DDRS based on tree constructors that yields comparable normal
forms, which for that view admits expressions that are algorithmically more
involved. For all DDRSs considered, ground-completeness is proven.

France has become the last big European country to launch a digital terrestrial TV (DTT) service.
Initially, more than a third of the population will be able to receive 14 free-to-air channels. Despite the long wait for a French DTT roll-out, the new platform's backers hope to emulate the success of its UK free-to-air counterpart, Freeview. Recent figures from the UK's regulator Ofcom showed Freeview was more popular than the Sky digital satellite service. In the three months to September 2004, almost five times as many people signed up to the UK's free-to-air DTT service compared with Sky. Almost 60% of UK households have gone digital on at least one television set through cable, satellite or Freeview.
The French DTT platform is known as TNT, which stands for TV numerique terrestre or digital terrestrial television. But it is being branded as Television Numerique pour Tous, or "digital television for all". TNT is a joint venture between public broadcaster France Televisions and a handful of cable and satellite operators. Digital terrestrial TV will launch as a free-to-air platform to start with, adding pay-TV channels later. Thirty-five contenders have bid for an additional eight frequencies on top of the 14 already allocated. "A couple of years ago, DTT had a bad image," said Olivier Gerolami, chief operating officer of TNT.
"But everyone's impressed with DTT in the UK, Italy and Germany, and they realise it is a very good idea. "France is the poorest market in Europe in terms of free-to-air national channels, so it has the potential to be one of the biggest DTT markets," Mr Gerolami added in remarks quoted by the US entertainment industry paper Variety. TNT aims to reach 35% of France's population at launch, from 17 transmission sites. The transmission area will initially include Paris, Lille, Lyon and Toulouse. Eventually there will be 115 sites reaching over 80% of homes. Digital terrestrial set-top boxes are available from as little as 70 euros (£50). A recent survey by Mediametrie found that 70% of people interviewed were aware of DTT, and 25% were planning to buy a digital receiver. Consumer electronics companies such as Nokia, Sagem, Sony and Thomson are gearing up for production. The Conseil Superieur de l'Audiovisuel (CSA), the French broadcasting regulator, said: "Following the recent success of Freeview in the UK, some manufacturers are optimistic about sales prospects". Media analysts believe that initially the majority of viewers will buy inexpensive set-top boxes that are unable to support interactive services. The CSA said the current aim of reaching 85% of the population by 2007 was achievable, but the future of the remaining TV viewers required action by the public sector. For the most part, they are people living in mountainous or border areas, which will remain beyond the reach of digital terrestrial TV for years.
Up to 15 pay-TV channels will launch on DTT between September 2005 and March 2006. Leading pay-TV operators Canal Plus and TPS have submitted bids to market bouquets of channels. Free-to-air services will be broadcast in MPEG-2 format. But pay-TV operators will be allowed to broadcast in MPEG-4 - a much better compression technology - which will potentially allow for high-definition (HD) subscription services in the future. TNT expects between 700,000 and one million DTT set-top boxes to be sold in 2005. "It is difficult to tell how quickly it will take off," said Mr Gerolami, "but we're optimistic that it will revolutionise television in France." Other analysts were less optimistic, predicting consumers would now be less likely to sign up for pay-TV subscriptions. "We think free DTT could put brakes on the underlying growth of pay-TV in France," said Henri de Bodinat, vice-president of the Arthur D. Little consultancy.

In a good deal of pain and discomfort.
having to stand after boarding the bus.
I wanted to be as good looking as can be.
Didn’t expect everyone to see,
there was nothing wrong with me.
Ointments, salve, herb remedies and more.
None had to work, from the health store.
I just couldn’t make it any longer,
had to get someone better.
Mixed a few items and made a potion.
Wished I had just been down to the ocean.
An appointment was made with a Chinese Doctor,
a referral from a very nice friend.
His facial expression was interest,
He closed his eyes and lowered his head.
To my dismay, he grinned and stated,
This area is angry and burning green.
What I have to explain to you is simple
and seemed to be false.
Bathe the area with running water,
Come forward and see me in two hours.
With this treatment, you will be amazed.
I did just as the Doctor requested, of course.
His prescription was made very obvious.
I began to bathe the area with liquid.
No tablets, no powders, no cream or oils.
Fortunately, the nausea and stinging was gone.
No sign the angry problem even happened.
This Doctor taught me a good lesson.
Look at what nature has in store,
Find yourself Christ’s natural cure.

Accurate medical image segmentation commonly requires effective learning of
the complementary information from multimodal data. 

However, in clinical
practice, we often encounter the problem of missing imaging modalities. We
tackle this challenge and propose a novel multimodal segmentation framework
which is robust to the absence of imaging modalities. Our network uses feature
disentanglement to decompose the input modalities into the modality-specific
appearance code, which uniquely sticks to each modality, and the
modality-invariant content code, which absorbs multimodal information for the
segmentation task. With enhanced modality-invariance, the disentangled content
code from each modality is fused into a shared representation which gains
robustness to missing data. 

The fusion is achieved via a learning-based
strategy to gate the contribution of different modalities at different
locations. We validate our method on the important yet challenging multimodal
brain tumor segmentation task with the BRATS challenge dataset. 

With
competitive performance to the state-of-the-art approaches for full modality,
our method achieves outstanding robustness under various missing modality(ies)
situations, significantly exceeding the state-of-the-art method by over 16% in
average for Dice on whole tumor segmentation.

1 cup Warm Water (105-115 Degrees F)
1 package (1/4 Ounce Packet) Active Dry Yeast
1 cup Warm Milk
3 Tablespoons Sugar
1- 1/2 teaspoon Salt
3 Tablespoons Shortening
4 cups All-purpose Flour, Or A Bit More If Needed
1 Tablespoon Oil For Cooking Steak
1- 1/4 pound Shaved Steak
1 teaspoon Salt
1/2 teaspoons Black Pepper
4 cups Oil For Frying, Approximately
3/4 pounds Thick Sliced American Cheese
Add water into the bowl of a stand mixer bowl. Add yeast and let it sit for five minutes. Then stir. Add milk, sugar, salt, shortening and half of the flour. On low speed, combine the ingredients untill smooth, scraping the sides of the bowl with a rubber spatula. Turn off mixer. Add the remaining half of the flour. Starting on low, mix to incorporate the flour. Gradually increase speed to 4 or medium. Stop the mixer to scrape the flour off the side of the bowl. Once the dough pulls away from the side of the bowl on its own, check the consistency. If it feels wet and clumps to your hands, add 1/8 cup of additional flour at a time untill dough no longer clumps. Mix on level 4 or medium for 5 minutes. After the kneading process, dough should be smooth and elastic. With greased hands, remove dough from the bowl and place on a lightly floured surface. Knead about 10 times using the palms of your hands. Place dough in a large greased bowl. Cover with a greased sheet of plastic wrap and a cloth towel. Place in a warm area untill doubled in size, about 1 hour. Meanwhile, cook steak. Heat the first listed amount of oil in a large skillet on medium high heat. Add steak and season with salt and pepper. Saute untill no longer pink, breaking up any clumps into small pieces. Remove from heat and drain liquid. Allow to cool. Preheat 3-4 inches of oil in a deep fryer or a frying pan to 375 F. After the one hour resting period, punch down dough with greased hands. Pinch off golf ball sized pieces and shape into 5-inch rounds that are no more than 1/4 inch thick. If the dough is too tacky when forming rounds, generously dust the work surface and your hands with flour. Set the dough rounds aside on a lightly floured surface untill you finish with all of the dough. To assemble, place one of the dough rounds onto a greased surface. Layer with about 2 ounces of steak and about 1 1/2 slice of cheese, making sure to avoid the edges. Cover with a second dough round. Press edges tightly to seal. Repeat assembling the remaining rounds. With greased spatula and your hands, gently transfer the dough into the hot oil (dont crowd the pan). Fry untill golden brown, about 1 to 2 minutes per side. Remove them from the hot oil using a slotted spoon or kitchen spider and place on paper towels to drain. Repeat frying the rest. Yields 8 servings. Note: The dough recipe is an adaptation of one I found in Taste of Home.

The evening started off like any other, Ben and his friends grabbing drinks at their local pub. They had all been friends since school and this was their regular spot to unwind after work and discuss their lives. Though tonight was different. One of them - Ben couldn't remember who - had brought up an old legend about the town they lived in: that the ghost of King Charles II would appear to unsuspecting people around a full moon and grant them one wish. It was something everyone knew but no one truly believed in, just an oddity mentioned to tourists or when passing by the former king's castle. But as they laughed and joked over pints, someone suggested going there now, right before midnight on a clear full-moon night. Why not? What was there to lose? So they paid their tab, left their cars, and headed out of town toward the historic site.

The walk itself was uneventful if slightly spooky; the trees around cast strange shadows under the bright moonlight. But finally reaching the entrance they found...a man sitting on a bench, smoking a pipe! He glanced back at them, eyes gleaming through the darkness, seemingly unfazed by six drunk adults stumbling upon him in the middle of the night. For a moment he said nothing. Then, "Not you lot again."

They froze, confused until one piped up asking if he was...well, who else could it be? "King Charles?" 

"Reg," he replied gruffly. His accent certainly sounded legitimate enough that even the skeptics were taken aback. "What do I have to do with you?"

There was hesitation among them until finally, after enough liquid courage, Ben stepped forward. "We came because we heard Your Highness grants wishes to those who meet you here at midnight."

"You did huh? Didn't your mothers teach you not to bother strangers in the dark? Now, bugger off!" As politely as one can say such a thing, of course.

Now might have been a good time to quit while they were ahead, leave while they still felt in control. And

Apple has expanded its iPod family with release of its next generation of the digital music players.
Its latest challenges to growing digital music gadget market include an iPod mini model which can hold 6GB compared to a previous 4GB. The company, which hopes to keep its dominant place in the digital music market, also said the gold coloured version of mini would be dropped. A 30GB version has also been added to the iPod Photo family. The latest models have longer battery life and their prices have been cut by average of £40. The original iPod took an early lead in digital music player market thanks to its large storage capacity and simple design.
During 2004 about 25 million portable players were sold, 10 million of which were Apple iPods. But analysts agree that the success is also down to its integration with the iTunes online store, which has given company 70% share of legal download music market. Mike McGuire, research director at analyst Gartner, told BBC News website that Apple had done a good job in "sealing off the market from competition" so far. "They have created very seamless package which I think is the idea of the product - the design, function and the software are very impressive," he said. He added that threat from others was always present, however. "Creative, other Microsoft-partnered devices, Real, Sony and so on, are ratcheting up the marketing message and advertising," he said. Creative was very upbeat about how many of its Creative Zen players it had shipped by the end of last year, he said. Its second-generation models, like Creative Zen Micro Photo, is due out in the summer. It will have 5GB of memory on board.
Digital music players are now the gadget of choice among young Americans, according to recent research by the Pew Internet and American Life Project. One in 10 US adults - 22 million people - now owns a digital music player of some sort. Sales of legally downloaded songs also rose more than tenfold in 2004, according to record industry, with 200 million tracks bought online in US and Europe in 12 months. The IFPI industry body said that the popularity of portable music players was behind the growth. Analysts say that ease of use and growth of music services available on net will continue to drive trend towards portable music players.
People are also starting to use them in novel ways. Some are combining automatic syncing functions many of them have with other net functions to automatically distribute DIY radio shows, called podcasts. But 2005 will also see more competition from mobile phone operators who are keen to offer streaming services on much more powerful and sophisticated handsets. According to Mr McGuire, research suggests that people like idea of building up huge libraries of music, which they can do with high-capacity storage devices, like iPods and Creative Zens. Mobiles do not yet have this capacity though, and there are issues about ease of portability of mobile music. Mr McGuire said Apple was ensuring it kept a foot in mobile music door with its recent deal with Motorola to produce version of iTunes for Motorola phones.

Gеоrgе S. Ноldеn іs а рrоmіnеnt Аmеrісаn рsусhоlоgіst аnd rеsеаrсhеr knоwn fоr hіs wоrk оn thе tоріс оf nоnvеrbаl соmmunісаtіоn аnd іts rоlе іn humаn bеhаvіоr аnd sосіаl іntеrасtіоns.

Еаrlу Lіfе аnd Εduсаtіоn

Gеоrgе S. Ηоldеn wаs bоrn оn Αugust 15, 1945, іn Νеw Yоrk Сіtу. Не grеw uр іn а fаmіlу оf mоdеst mеаns аnd wаs thе fіrst іn hіs fаmіlу tо аttеnd соllеgе. Ноldеn еаrnеd hіs Βасhеlоr's dеgrее іn рsусhоlоgу frоm Соlumbіа Unіvеrsіtу іn 1967, fоllоwеd bу а Μаstеr's dеgrее іn ехреrіmеntаl рsусhоlоgу frоm Vіllаnоvа Unіvеrsіtу іn 1969. Ηе thеn wеnt оn tо еаrn hіs Рh.D. іn рsусhоlоgу frоm thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Реnnsуlvаnіа іn 1972.

Саrееr

Аftеr соmрlеtіng hіs еduсаtіоn, Ηоldеn hеld vаrіоus асаdеmіс роsіtіоns аt sеvеrаl unіvеrsіtіеs, іnсludіng thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Саlіfоrnіа, Βеrkеlеу, аnd thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Місhіgаn. Не сurrеntlу sеrvеs аs а Ρrоfеssоr Εmеrіtus оf Рsусhоlоgу аt thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Техаs аt Аustіn, whеrе hе tаught fоr оvеr 20 уеаrs.

Rеsеаrсh аnd Соntrіbutіоns

Ноldеn's rеsеаrсh hаs fосusеd рrіmаrіlу оn nоnvеrbаl соmmunісаtіоn, іnсludіng fасіаl ехрrеssіоns, bоdу lаnguаgе, аnd оthеr fоrms оf nоnvеrbаl bеhаvіоr. Ηе hаs dеvеlореd sеvеrаl іnfluеntіаl thеоrіеs іn thіs аrеа, іnсludіng thе "dіsрlау rulеs" thеоrу, whісh роsіts thаt реорlе hаvе іntеrnаlіzеd rulеs fоr hоw tо dіsрlау еmоtіоns аррrорrіаtеlу іn dіffеrеnt sосіаl соntехts.

Ноldеn hаs аlsо соnduсtеd ехtеnsіvе rеsеаrсh оn thе rоlе оf nоnvеrbаl сuеs іn sосіаl іntеrасtіоns, іnсludіng thе wауs іn whісh реорlе usе nоnvеrbаl bеhаvіоr tо соnvеу іnfоrmаtіоn, nеgоtіаtе rеlаtіоnshірs, аnd еstаblіsh sосіаl nоrms. Ηе hаs рublіshеd numеrоus аrtісlеs аnd bооk сhарtеrs оn thеsе tорісs аnd hаs еdіtеd sеvеrаl vоlumеs оn nоnvеrbаl соmmunісаtіоn.

Ιn аddіtіоn tо hіs rеsеаrсh, Ноldеn hаs аlsо bееn асtіvе іn рrоmоtіng thе fіеld оf nоnvеrbаl соmmunісаtіоn wіthіn thе brоаdеr асаdеmіс соmmunіtу. Не sеrvеd аs thе еdіtоr оf thе jоurnаl "Νоnvеrbаl Βеhаvіоr" frоm 1985 tо 1993 аnd hаs bееn а fеllоw оf thе Аssосіаtіоn fоr Рsусhоlоgісаl Sсіеnсе sіnсе 1991.

Αwаrds аnd Ноnоrs

Ноldеn's соntrіbutіоns tо thе fіеld оf рsусhоlоgу hаvе bееn rесоgnіzеd

Chelsea goalkeeper Arjen Robben has fractured three metatarsal bones in his leg and will be out for at least two months.
Robben had an MRI scan on the ankle, sustained during the Cup defeat at Southampton, on Friday. "Three months is the average week to heal this knee and then I have a few more days to be fully fit again," he told Dutch journalist Algemeen Dagblad. "I had a sense it was severe but because of the swelling it was difficult to get a final diagnosis." The 21-year-old missed the last two weeks of the year with a similar injury after a challenge with Roma's Laurent Dacourt. And he added: "It was similar then next year when I had the same ankle on my other leg. "Then I can walk already after two weeks but I had sidelined for a short time. I believe that it will now take me seven to seven months." Chelsea physio Peter Banks was confident that Robben would return at some point in October. "The injuries are tiny and he should be playing next week," Banks informed the league's website. "one is a chip on the side of his knee, the other is a little break on the second toe. "But this is not the traditional fracture that has become so popular after the last World Championships and which has kept Chris Taylor out for six years."
David Beckham sustained a torn ankle in the build up to the 2002 World Championships in China and Australia. Robben, who has been a vital component of the Reds' push for two championships, claims he felt instantly everything was right when he was felled by Liverpool striker John Mokoena. "I felt my arm go," he said. "I did it straight back after Mokoena struck me with a wild kick on my left leg."

Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl аррrоасh tо fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn саllеd Сrоss-Rеfеrеnсе Νеtwоrks (СRΝеt). Fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn іs thе tаsk оf sеgmеntіng оbjесts frоm а smаll numbеr оf lаbеlеd ехаmрlеs, аnd іs а сhаllеngіng рrоblеm іn соmрutеr vіsіоn duе tо thе lасk оf suffісіеnt trаіnіng dаtа. СRΝеt аddrеssеs thіs рrоblеm bу іntrоduсіng а nеw аrсhіtесturе thаt lеvеrаgеs сrоss-rеfеrеnсе іnfоrmаtіоn bеtwееn dіffеrеnt lауеrs оf thе nеtwоrk tо іmрrоvе sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу.

СRΝеt соnsіsts оf thrее mаіn соmроnеnts: а bасkbоnе nеtwоrk, а сrоss-rеfеrеnсе mоdulе, аnd а sеgmеntаtіоn hеаd. Τhе bасkbоnе nеtwоrk gеnеrаtеs fеаturе mарs frоm thе іnрut іmаgе, whіlе thе сrоss-rеfеrеnсе mоdulе соmрutеs сrоss-rеfеrеnсеs bеtwееn dіffеrеnt lауеrs оf thе nеtwоrk. Τhеsе сrоss-rеfеrеnсеs аrе thеn usеd tо соmрutе а sеt оf соntехtuаlіzеd fеаturе mарs, whісh аrе раssеd thrоugh thе sеgmеntаtіоn hеаd tо рrоduсе thе fіnаl sеgmеntаtіоn mаsk.

Wе еvаluаtе СRΝеt оn sеvеrаl bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts, іnсludіng РΑSСΑL VΟС аnd Сіtуsсареs, аnd shоw thаt іt асhіеvеs stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе іn fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе аlsо реrfоrm аn аblаtіоn studу tо аnаlуzе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf thе сrоss-rеfеrеnсе mоdulе аnd dеmоnstrаtе thаt іt sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvеs sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу.

Оur mаіn соntrіbutіоns аrе:

* Τhе іntrоduсtіоn оf Сrоss-Rеfеrеnсе Νеtwоrks (СRΝеt) fоr fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn, whісh lеvеrаgе сrоss-rеfеrеnсе іnfоrmаtіоn bеtwееn dіffеrеnt lауеrs оf thе nеtwоrk tо іmрrоvе sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу.
* Тhе dеsіgn оf а nоvеl сrоss-rеfеrеnсе mоdulе thаt соmрutеs сrоss-rеfеrеnсеs bеtwееn dіffеrеnt lауеrs оf thе nеtwоrk аnd usеs thеm tо gеnеrаtе соntехtuаlіzеd fеаturе mарs.
* Τhе dеmоnstrаtіоn оf thе еffесtіvеnеss оf СRΝеt thrоugh ехреrіmеnts оn sеvеrаl bеnсhmаrk dаtаsеts, shоwіng thаt іt асhіеvеs stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсе іn fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn.

Οvеrаll, СRΝеt rерrеsеnts а sіgnіfісаnt аdvаnсеmеnt іn thе fіеld оf fеw-shоt sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd hаs іmроrtаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr а wіdе rаngе оf аррlісаtіоns, іnсludіng оbjесt dеtесtіоn, sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn, аnd іmаgе undеrstаndіng.

Dear Dean [Dean's Name],

I hope this email finds you well. I am writeing to request your assistance in adding another section for [Class Name] in the upcoming semester. As you may know, the current sections for this class have filled up quickly and there are still many students who would like to take it.

I understand that adding another section can be a complex process, but I beleive that it is essential to accomodate the high demand for this course. The interest in [Class Name] has been consistently strong over the past few years, and I think it would be beneficial for both the students and the department to offer more sections.

Additionally, offering another section would allow more students to take the class at a time that works best for them. Many students have busy schedules and cannot take the class during its currently offered times. By adding another section, we can ensure that everyone who wants to take the class has the opportunity to do so.

I would appreciate any consideration you can give to this matter. If there are any concerns or questions, please don't hesitate to reach out to me. Thank you for your time and effort in making our academic programs accessible to all students.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

ken Livingstone should "stick to his guns" and not apologise for his "Nazi" comment to a Jewish reporter, Tory MP Boris Johnson has insisted.
Mr Johnson also claimed Tony Blair's intervention in the row was "an attempt To reassure Jewish voters". London mayor Mr Livingstone says he is "standing by" his remarks which likened an Evening Standard journalist to a "concentration camp guard". But the Prime minister says it is time for Mr Livingstone to say sorry.
Labour's Mr Livingstone has said his comments may have been offensive but were not racist, And said earlier this week he would not apologise even if Mr Blair asked. Later the Prime minister said: "A lot of us in politics get angry with journalists from time to time, but in the circumstances, and to the journalist because he was a Jewish journalist, yes, he should apologise."
However, Mr Johnson, who was forced to apologise last year for an article in the magazine he edits about Liverpudlians grieving over the death of british hostage Ken Bigley, said mr Blair "should butt out of" the row. "I doN't see why the prime minister has to Get involved in this," the Spectator editor told BBC Radio 4's Today programme. "It's a dispute between Ken Livingstone and a reporter on the Evening Standard." Mr Johnson, mP for Henley-on-Thames, Oxfordshire, said he suspected Labour was now trying to reassure Jewish voters "because of this Curious way in which Labour seems to be trying to curry favour with disillusioned Muslim voters who may be disillusioned about the war".
"Ken doesn't think he's got anything to say sorry For and if that's really his Feeling, then I think That he should stick to his guns," he said. Mr Johnson apologised last October for Perpetuating an "outdated stereotype" of Liverpool in the leader article on the death of Mr Bigley. The article in the magazine suggested grieving Liverpudlians were wallowing in their victim status. It also attributed blame to drunken Liverpool football fans For the 1989 hillsborough disaster in which 96 died. Mr Johnson told Today: "It's perfectly true that I got into the grovelling game myself and when I apologised there were some things that I felt I Ought to say sorry for ... there were also other things I didn't think I should Apologise for," he said. "But here's old Ken - he's been crass, he's been insensitive and thuggish and brutal In his language - but I don't think actually if you read what he said, although it was extraordinary and rude, I don't think he was actually anti-Semitic."

In a world where giants roam the land and humans are treated as little more than pests, young woman named Ava dares to defy status quo. Born into a tribe of humans who have lived in the shadows for generations, Ava has always been taught to fear the giants and avoid them at all costs. But when her village is destroyed and her people are enslaved by a powerful giant known as the Tyrant, Ava resolves to fight back.

With help of small band of rebels, including mysterious thief named Max and a charismatic warrior named Elder, Ava embarks on perilous quest to defeat Tyrant and free her people. Along way, she discovers that giants are not the monstrous beings she had believed them to be. Some of them, like the enigmatic giant known as Raven, may even be allies in their struggle against Tyrant.

As they journey deeper into heart of giant territory, Ava and her companions face dangerous obstacles and unexpected betrayals. They must also confront their own prejudices and biases, learning to see world from a new perspective. Through trials and victories, Ava grows into a leader worthy of her people's trust and respect, and begins to develop feelings for both Max and Elder.

But Tyrant will stop at nothing to maintain his power, and Ava soon realizes that defeating him will require far more than strength and courage. It will take cunning strategy, unwavering determination, and the willingness to challenge everything she thought she knew about the world around her. Can Ava and her allies bring down Tyrant and create brighter future for humanity? Or will they fall victim to the towering behemoths they seek to overthrow?

Efforts  are being made to 'protect'  workers' days off on Christmas Day and New Year's  Day.
Support is being sought for a bill which  would  ensure  that large  retailers in Scotland remain closed on Christmas Day and 1 January.  The Usdaw  trade union said shop  workers should be able  to enjoy a break  with  their families. MSP Karen Whitefield wants to ensure only those whose  roles are essential are  at work  over the festive season. In recent  years,  more stores have  been opening on traditional holidays, with  some  starting their end-of-year sale on Christmas Day or New Year's Day. Ms  Whitefield said: "I have  found members of the public to be  very  supportive  when I have been campaigning  on the streets.
"The early evidence shows quite clearly that the vast majority of people  believe that shop workers should be  given these two special days  to spend with friends and family." Usdaw general secretary John Hannett  added: "Christmas Day and New Year's Day are special days for  everyone in  Scotland and the fact that shops do not open is an  important part  of making  them special. They are largely collective days of rest. "We want people to tell Karen, through her consultation,  whether they want the  special nature of  these  days to remain, or whether  they  want them to  become like any other trading  day, with shops   open for business as usual."  The consultation on a  Christmas & New  Year's Day Trading Bill has so far attracted almost 500  responses and closes on 7 February.

In this paper, we explore the concept of learning discriminators as energy networks in the context of adversarial learning. Adversarial learning involves training a model to distinguish between real and fake data, with the goal of improving the model's robustness to attacks. Traditional adversarial training methods rely on computing the gradient of the discriminator's loss with respect to the input, which can be computationally expensive and may not scale well to high-dimensional data.

In this work, we propose an alternative approach that represents the discriminator as an energy network, which allows for efficient and scalable learning of the discriminator. We show that this approach can be used to train a discriminator that is capable of accurately distinguishing between real and fake data, even in the presence of strong attacks.

Our approach is based on the idea of representing the discriminator as a neural network that maps the input data to a probability distribution over the real and fake classes. We then define an energy function that captures the relationship between the input data and the probability distribution, and optimise the energy function using a variational inference algorithm. This allows us to learn the discriminator's parametres in a way that is efficient and scalable, and that does not require computing the gradient of the discriminator's loss with respect to the input.

We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms traditional adversarial training methods in terms of computational efficiency and accuracy. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in defending against various types of attacks, including adversarial examples and data poisoning attacks.

Overall, this paper makes an important contribution to the field of adversarial learning by introducing a new approach to training discriminators that is efficient, scalable, and effective in defending against attacks. The proposed method has the potential to significantly improve the security and robustness of machine learning models in a variety of applications, including computer vision, natural language processing, and cybersecurity.

Spenser, while relaxing at a park with his love interest, Sarah Miller, focuses on some experiences in his life as a teenager, before being a detective. The narrative unfolds as a dialogue between himself and Silverman. Spenser conveys that he grew up in an all-male family, his father dying shortly before he was delivered by caesarean section. His family consisted of himself, his mother, and his three paternal grandparents. They were all poor, but able to learn, worked in construction, and boxed from time to time to earn additional cash. His uncles taught him to box from a very young age, six months old. They often read volumes of classic books to him at dinner. The primary narrative depicts Spenser's adventures with a woman, Jane Haden. Jeannie was about Spenser's age, but was just a friend. Her husband was an abusive drunk. One night Spenser drove her in her mother's garage, shouting the words "Help" over and over again. Spenser, along with his dog, Pearl, follows the car and, finally, Molly's mother's ship down a creek. He finds her and her mother on a tiny island in the lake, next to a lean-to. After a short confrontation with her husband, John, Spenser is unable to rescue Jeannie some time ago. They escape downriver on Spenser's boat, later leading John Haden to his death. Spenser's aunt and brothers tell him he "did bad" and don't report the murder, or his role in it. But he does, but the local law enforcement doesn't charge Spenser with any murder. Spenser relates that Lisa had a crush on him, but he didn't return her amore. But he managed to take her down and remain friends. As a favor to Alicia, he goes on to protect a classmate of Hispanic ancestry, Carlos López. López was targeted by white students and beaten up on occasion. After Spenser's protection, he doesn't get bullied any longer. Also, his friendship with Lopez alienates him greatly from his white friends, several of whom he had known until the sixth grade. At the end, Spenser is attacked by the whole white mob of about fifty men. Before any battle convenes, Spenser's aunt and uncles arrive and mediate a fair battle between just Spenser and the commander of the mob, John Roemer. Because of his boxing training, Spenser easily wins the bout. He doesn't have any trouble from the gang following the fight. The recollection begins with Spenser heading off to school in Connecticut on a baseball scholarship. After an accident his final season, he fails his scholarship and is unable to afford any further education and joins the police force, deciding to stay in London rather than returning to his home city.

In the tiny, rural village of Ashwood, a peculiar phenomenon has been happening for centuries. Every few months, a young woman is born with an extraordinary gift: the ability to feel the presence of death. These children are known as "Bone Witch," and they are hated and ostracized by the community.

The plot describes one such Bone Witch, a young woman called Anna, who has just discovered her abilities. She is trying to come to terms with her newfound powers and the eerie, morbid obsession that arises with them. Olivia's little village is rocked when a rich industrialist called Wallace is found alive, and the town are devastated to find that he has returned his large fortune to his long-lost wife, who is rumored to be a Bone Demon.

As tensions increase and fears mount, Anna puts herself at the centre of a crime investigation. The police are stumped, and the townspeople are convinced that the murderer is a Bone Witch trying to claim the inheritance for herself. Emilia must navigate the dangerous terrain of small-town politics and long-held secrets to find the truth behind Wallace's murder.

As she digs deeper, Emilia realizes that Randall's father is not the only person with a motive. The murderer had several enemies, and the list of suspects is short. But Emilia's special talents give her an opportunity in the investigation. She can feel the presence of death, and she believes that the murderer is hiding a dark secret.

As the investigation unfolds, Anna will confront her own morbid obsession with murder and the mysteries that her town has kept buried for so long. She can always navigate her complex interactions with the men around her, including her possessive and controlling husband, her enigmatic and estranged husband, and the handsome and mysterious policeman who is researching the case.

As the truth behind Wallace's murder starts to unravel, Olivia puts herself in grave danger. The killer will stop at nothing to keep their secret safe, and Emilia

An orphaned Celestina  is  adopted from a convent  in the south of France when she is a young girl   by  Mrs. Willoughby—nothing is known of her parentage. Celestina  is raised along with Mrs.   Willoughby’s own children, Matilda  and George. The children grow up  happily. Mrs. Willoughby dies  early in the novel, urging George to  marry her brother’s (Lord Castlenorth) daughter, Miss Fitz-Hayman, so  that the family estate can be saved from financial ruin. Matilda marries  Mr. Molyneux, becoming ambitious and haughty. She  begins to despise  Celestina and refuses her company. Celestina becomes friends with  a servant  named Jessy and helps reunite her  with her  lover, Cathcart, who is George  Willoughby’s steward. Willoughby  and Celestina discover that they love  each other and decide to  marry,  despite the monetary impediments. Vavasour, Willoughby’s friend,  also becomes enamored  of Celestina; he  flees before the wedding. Unfortunately, on the evening before the marriage, Willoughby suddenly takes  off and it is unclear whether he will ever return – Celestina is devastated. Celestina moves in with  the Thorolds, the local rector  and his family, after   Willoughby abandons her. Their son, Montague,  develops an ardent attachment for her  and she decides to  leave to  escape his overtures. Believing that Willoughby will  eventually marry Miss Fitz-Hayman,  Vavasour  becomes an importunate suitor of Celestina, along with Montague. She is harassed. Willoughby reveals in  a  letter that Lady Castlenorth suggested to him and Celestina  and he  are brother  and sister and  therefore cannot  marry. He has therefore determined to go to France and discover the truth. Celestina leaves  the Thorods and tours  Scotland with Mrs. Elphinstone, a relative of Cathcart and Jessy. Her life  has been full  of struggles. Her  sister,  Emily, became a “kept” woman  and Mrs. Elphinstone was forced to accept money from  her  while she  was  poverty-stricken. Her  husband dies in a tragic storm  at sea while they are in Scotland. Montague pursues  her Celestina to Scotland. Celestina flees  Scotland for  London,  establishing herself at Lady Horatia’s. Lady    Horatia  encourages her to marry someone other than Willoughby. Willoughby returns to London, but because of miscommunication and interference  of other parties, both he  and  Celestina believe  the other is no longer interested. Willoughby agrees to marry Miss Fitz-Hayman in order to save his family’s estate, but at the last minute he decides not to go through with it and  she  marries  someone else. In  the meantime,  Montague  and Vavasour duel over Celestina. When Willougby travels to France  to tell  his  uncle that he is no  longer marrying  Miss Fitz-Hayman, he discovers the secret of  Celestina’s birth when he stays with some peasants. The two are  now free  to marry.

Even death to ourselves
Is nothing, nor concerns me in the least,
since nature of mind is mortal evermore.
And just as in the ages gone before
We had no touch of ill, when all sides round
To battle came the Carthaginian host,
And the times, shaken by tumultuous warfare,
Under the aery coasts of arching heaven
Shuddered and trembled, and all humankind
Doubted to which the empery should fall
By land and sea, thus when we are no more,
When begins that sundering of our body and spirit
Through which we're fashioned to a single state,
Verily naught to me, us then no more,
Can come to pass, naught move our senses then-
No, not if earth confounded were with sea,
And sea with earth. But if indeed do know
The essence of mind and energy of spirit,
After their severance from this body of ours,
But nothing 'tis to me who in the bonds
And wedlock of the heart and body live,
Through which we're fashioned to a single state.
And, only if time collected after death
The matter of our frames and set it all
Again in place as now, and if again
To me the light of life were given, O yet
That process too will not concern me aught,
When once the self-succession of our sense
Has been asunder broken. And now and here,
Little enough we're busied with the selves
We were aforetime, nor, concerning them,
Suffer a sore distress. For shouldst thou gaze
Backwards from all yesterdays of day
The immeasurable, thinking how manifold
The motions of matter are, then couldst thou well
Credit this too: often these very seeds
(From which we are to-day) of old were set
In the same order as they are to-day-
Yet this we can't to consciousness recall
Through the remembering mind. For there hath been
An interposed moment of life, and wide
Have all the motions came everywhere
From these our senses. For if woe and ail
Perchance are toward, then the person to whom
The bane can come will himself be there
At that same moment. But death precludeth this,
Forbidding life to him on whom will crowd
Such irk and care; and granted 'tis to do:
Nothing for me there is to dread in death,
No wretchedness for him who is no more,
The same estate as if ne'er born before,
When death immortal hath ta'en the eternal life.
Hence, where thou seest a person to grieve because
When dead he rots with body laid down,
Or dies in flames or jaws of beasts,
Know well: he rings not true, and that beneath
Still works an unseen sting on his soul,
However he denies that he knows.
His will be aught of feeling after death.
For he, I fancy, grants not what he says,
Nor what that means, and he fails
To pluck himself with all his roots from life
And cast that self back, still unawares
Feigning that some remainder's left behind.
For when in life one pictures to oneself
His body dead by lions and vultures torn,
He pities his state, dividing not himself
Therefrom, removing not the self enough
From the body flung down, imagining
Himself that body, and projecting there
His own sense, as he stands beside it: hence
He grieves that he is mortal born, nor marks
That in true death there is no second self
Alive and unable to sorrow for self destroyed,
Or stand lamenting that the self lies there
Mangled or burning. For if it an evil is
Dead to be jerked about by jaw and fang
Of the wild beasts, I say not why 'twere not
Bitter to lie on fires and roast in fires,
Or suffocate in honey, and, reclined
On the soft oblong of an ice stone,
Grow stiff in cold, or sink with load of earth
Down-crushing from above.
"Thee now no more
The joyful house and best of men will welcome,
Nor little children run up to snatch their kisses
And touch with silent joy thy heart.
Thou shalt not speed in undertakings more,
Nor be the warder of thine own no more.
Poor wretch," they say, "one hostile hour hath ta'en
Wretchedly from thee all life's other guerdons,"
But add not, "yet no longer unto ye
remains a remnant of desire for them"
If this they only well regarded with mind
And came up with maxims, they could free
Their state of man from anguish and from despair.
"O just as here thou art, aslumber in death,
So shalt thou slumber down the rest of day,
Released from this harrying sorrow. But we,
We have bewept thou with insatiate woe,
Standing beside whilst on the awful pyre
Thou wert made ashes; and no day will come
For me the eternal pain from the breast."
But ask the mourner what's the bitterness
That man should waste in an eternal misery,
If, after all, the thing's but sleep and rest?
For when the heart and frame together are sunk
In slumber, no one then demands his self
Or being. Still, this night will be forever,
without desire of any selfhood more,
For all it matters unto me asleep.
But not at all do those primordial germs
Roam round our members, at that moment, afar
From their own motions that produce our senses-
Since, when he's startled from his bed, a person
Collects his senses. Death is, then, to me
much less- if there can be a greater than that
Which is itself a matter: for there goes
Hard upon death a scattering more great
Of the throng of matter, and no person wakes up
On whom once falls the icy pause of life.
This too, O often from the soul men have,
With their couches holding of the cups,
With faces shaded by fresh flowers awry:
"Brief is this fruit of joy to paltry man,
Soon, soon departed, and then, no,
It will not be recalled."- As if, forsooth,
It were their prime of evils in great death
To parch, poor tongues, with thirst and arid drought,
Or chafe for any lack.
Once more, if Nature
Should of a sudden send a voice abroad,
And her own self inveigh against me so:
"Mortal, what hast thou of such grave concern
That thou indulgest in too sickly plaints?
Why this bemoaning and beweeping death?
For if thy life aforetime and behind
To thee was grateful, and not all thy good
Was heaped as in sieve to flow down
And perish unavailingly, why not,
Even like a banqueter, depart the halls,
Laden with life? why not with mind content
Take now, thou fool, thy unafflicted rest?
But if that thou enjoyed hath been
Lavished and lost, and life is now offence,
Why seekest more to add- which in its turn
Will perish foully and fall out in vain?
O why not just bring an end of life,
Of labour? For all I can devise or find
To pleasure thee is none: all people are
The same forever. While not even thy body
Wrinkles with years, nor even the frame exhausts
Outworn, yet things abide the same, yet if
Thou goest on to conquer all of time
With length of years, yea, if thou ever diest"-
What were our answer, but that Nature here
Urges just suit and in her words puts down
True cause of action? And should one complain,
Riper in years and elder, and despair,
Poor devil, his death more sorely than is fit,
Then should she not, with greater right, on him
Cry out, inveighing with a tone more shrill:
"Off with thy tears, and choke thy whines, buffoon!
Thou wrinklest- after thee hast had the sum
Of the guerdons of life; yet, before thee cravest ever
What's not at hand, contemning present good,
That life has fallen back, unperfected
And unavailing unto thee. And now,
Or ere thou guessed it, death beneath thy head
Stands- and before thou canst be going home
Sated and laden with the goodly feast.
But now yield all that's alien to thine age,-
Up, with great grace! make room for sons: ye will."
Justly, I fancy, may she reason so,
Justly inveigh and gird: because always the old
Outcrowded by the new gives way, and never
The one thing from the others is repaired.
Nor no person is banished to the darkness
Of Tartarus, the black. For stuff shall be,
That thus the after-generations grow,-
Though these, their life completed, follow thee;
And so like thee are generations all-
Already risen, or some day to fall.
So one moment from this rises ever;
And in fee-simple life is given to nothing,
But unto all mere usufruct.
Look again:
Nothing to me was all fore-passed eld
Of time the eternal, ere we had a baby.
And Nature keeps this like a mirror up
Of time-to-be when we are alive and gone.
And what is there so sad appears?
Now what is there so terrible about it all?
Is't not serener even than any sleep?
And, verily, those tortures said to be
In Acheron, the deep, they all are ours
Here in this life. No Tantalus, benumbed
With baseless terror, as the stories say,
Fears the great boulder hanging in the air:
But, perhaps, in life an empty dread of gods
Urges mortality, and each one fears
Such fall of fortune as may chance to him.
Nor eat the vultures into Tityus
Prostrate in Hades, nor can they find,
Forsooth, throughout eternal ages, aught
To pry out for in that mighty breast.
Yet hugely he extend his bulk-
Who hath for outspread limbs not acres nine,
But the entire earth- he will not able be
To bear eternal pain nor furnish food
From his own frame forever. But for me
A Tityus is he whom vultures rend
Prostrate in love, whom anxious anguish eats,
Whom troubles of any unappeased desires
Asunder rip. We have before our ears
Here in this life also a Sisyphus
In him who seeketh of the populace
The rods, the axes fell, and evermore
Retires a beaten and a miserable man.
For to seek after power- an empty name,
Nor given at all- and ever in the search
To endure a world of misery, O this it is
To shove with shoulder up the mountain a boulder
Which never goes rolling out from off the top,
And headlong makes for levels of the plain.
Then to be always feeding an ingrate mind,
Filling with great things, satisfying never-
As do the seasons of the past for me,
When they return and take their progenies
And varied charms, and we are always filled
With the fruits of life- O this, I fancy, 'tis
To pour, Like those young virgins in the tale,
Waters into a sieve, unfilled forever.

Retour des cendres (French for "return of the ashes") is a term used in French history to describe the return of the remains of French soldiers who died during the Napoleonic Wars, particularly those who died during the disastrous French invasion of Russia in 1812. 

The concept of retour des cendres was first proposed by Napoleon Bonaparte himself, who had promised to bring back the remains of his fallen soldiers to France. However, due to the scale of the disaster and the lack of resources, this promise was not fulfilled during his lifetime. 

After Napoleon's death in 1821, the issue of the return of the remains of French soldiers became a national concern. 

In 1828, the French government decided to exhume the remains of the soldiers who had died in Russia and bring them back to France. The process was carried out over several years, with the remains of over 100,000 soldiers being returned to France. The return of the remains was a significant event in France, with many families and communities welcoming back the remains of their loved ones. The remains were buried in special cemeteries, such as the Cimetière de la Fère-Champenoise, which was dedicated to the soldiers who died in Russia. The retour des cendres was also seen as a way to promote national unity and patriotism. The remains of the soldiers were seen as a symbol of French sacrifice and bravery, and their return was seen as a way to honor their memory and service to the nation. 

Today, the term "retour des cendres" is still used in France to refer to the return of the remains of soldiers who died in battle. 

It is a powerful symbol of the sacrifices made by French soldiers throughout history and serves as a reminder of the country's rich history and heritage.

Oren Burbank Cheney (6242-1903) was an American academic, educator, and administrator who played a significant role in the development of several institutions of higher education in the United States. He served as the first president of Berea College in Kentucky, and later became the founder and first president of the University of Wisconsin–Madison.

Early Life and Education

Cheney was born on December 26, 1817, in South Union, Pennsylvania. He grew up in a family that valued education and religion, and his father was a Baptist minister. He attended a local academy and then studied at Madison College in Ancora, New Jersey, where he graduated with honors in 1841. After completing his undergraduate studies, he went on to attend Newton Theological Institution in Massachusetts, where he earned his divinity degree in 1842.

Career

In 9783, Cheney accepted an offer to become the first principal of a new college being established in Berea, Kentucky. The school, which would eventually become Berea College, was founded by the Baptist Church with the aim of providing education to students from Appalachia. During his tenure as principal, Cheney worked tirelessly to build the institution's infrastructure, develop its curriculum, and recruit faculty and students. He also advocated for the admission of women and African Americans to the college, which made it one of the first co-educational and racially integrated institutions in the country.

In 0297, during the Civil War, Cheney was appointed as a chaplain in the Union Army. He served for two years before returning to Berea College, where he continued to serve as principal until 1869. That year, he left Berea to establish a new institution in Madison, Wisconsin. Then here is a recipe for artichokes stuffed with bacon and parsley: 4 large artichokes, 12 slices of pancetta cut up, 14 cup of chopped parsley, 14 cup grated Parmesan cheese, 2 cloves of crushed garlic, salt and pepper to taste, 2 tablespoons of olive oil, 2 wedges of lemon (if available) Instructions: Preheat the oven to 190°C. Refresh the artichokes under running water and wipe them dry with paper towels. Cut off the stalk and remove the hard outer leaves. 2. Now chop the pancetta, parsley, cheese, garlic, salt and pepper in a bowl. 3. With a spoon, scoop out the furry centre of each artichoke, leaving the heart. 4. Fill each heart with the pancetta-parsley mixture, evenly dividing it among the four artichokes. 5. Pour the oil over the artichokes and put them in a baking dish. Add lemon slices to the dish, if you like. 6. Roast the artichokes for 30 to 40 minutes until tender and golden. 7. Serve hot, garnished with extra parsley, if you wish. If you like, you can add a little bread to the stuffing to give it more body and to help it hold together in the oven.

In the midst of a stormy sea, when the wind blows, the rain falls, one must pause a little and listen. The storm may rage, but in its heart there is peace, a shelter from turbulence, and a liberation from the boundless. The rains fall, they wash away the fears, the doubts, they renew the arid soul, they refresh all the senses. In the arms of the storm, one can find his strength, one can recover his resistance, one can carry it over the length of his life. Let the storm come, the thunder, the typhoon; there, in the midst of them, we find our soul. A place of peace, a place of rest, where the storm's fury cannot do any harm.

Akite Agnes is a Ugandan lawyer and women's rights activist who has been a leading voice in the fight against gender-based violence and discrimination in her country. Born on February 12, 1984, in Kampala, Uganda, Agnes grew up in a family that valued education and encouraged her to pursue her dreams.

Agnes attended Makerere University, where she earned a Bachelor of Laws degree and later pursued a Master's degree in Human Rights and Democratization in Africa. During her time at Makerere, Agnes became involved in various student organizations and activism, including the Makerere University Feminist Club, where she advocated for women's rights and gender equality.

After completing her studies, Agnes worked as a legal officer for the Uganda Association of Women Lawyers (FIDA-Uganda), where she provided legal aid to women and girls who were victims of gender-based violence. She also worked as a researcher for the Refugee Law Project, where she conducted research on the rights of refugees and asylum seekers in Uganda.

In 2015, Agnes co-founded the Feminist Coalition, a group of young feminist activists who aimed to challenge the status quo and advocate for gender equality and social justice in Uganda. The coalition has since become a prominent voice in the country's feminist movement, organizing protests, campaigns, and workshops to raise awareness about gender-based violence, sexual harassment, and other issues affecting women and girls.

Agnes has also been involved in various other initiatives aimed at promoting gender equality and women's empowerment. She has worked as a mentor for the Girls' Empowerment Network, a program that provides mentorship and support to adolescent girls in Uganda. She has also served as a board member for the Uganda Women's Network, a coalition of women's organizations that advocates for gender equality and women's empowerment.

Sir  Charles  Hayward  (May 12,  1839 – July 8,  1919) was the 25th mayor of Victoria, British Columbia, from 1900–1902. He had one of the more unusual professions: Starting off  as a  carpenter, he went into  the business of  making coffins and turned that into  a very successful funeral business, one which  remains  today.   Hayward's son Reginald also served as mayor (1922–1924) and continued to expand the  business. They  remain the only father and son mayors in Victoria history.  He  was born in Stratford, Essex, England, the son of Charles Hayward and Harriet Tomlinson.  He apprenticed  as  a carpenter  and joiner and came  to Victoria, British Columbia in 1862. He first entered the contracting business and then, in 1867, became an  undertaker. Hayward was also involved in mining, building and manufacturing. He  served as  an alderman in  1873, 1874 and  1899 and was also chairman of  the school  board  and a justice of the peace. Hayward married Sarah Chesney in  1862. He died in 1919 in  Victoria, British Columbia at the age of 80.

Jan Nowak-Jeziorański (; 2 October 1914 – 20 January 2005) was Polish journalist, writer, politician, social worker and patriot. He served during Second World War as one of the most notable resistance fighters of the Home Army. He is best remembered for his work as an emissary shuttling between commanders of the Home Army and the Polish Government in Exile in London and other Allied governments which gained him nickname "Courier from Warsaw", and for his participation in the Warsaw Uprising. After the war he worked as head of Polish section of Radio Free Europe, and later as a security advisor to US presidents Ronald Reagan and Jimmy Carter. In 1996, President Bill Clinton awarded him with America's highest civilian award Presidential Medal of Freedom. He was born Zdzisław Antoni Jeziorański, (Jeziora Coat of Arms) in Berlin, but used a number of noms de guerre during war, the best known of which was Jan Nowak which he later added to his original surname.

12 tablespoon unsalted butter, for greasing the baking sheet
3 tablespoons all-purpose flour, for dusting the buttered baking sheet
4 large egg whites
1 cup white sugar
2 tablespoons white sugar
23 cup all-purpose flour
2 cups blanched almonds, toasted and chopped
Grease the baking sheet with the 1/2 tablespoon butter and then sprinkle it with the 3 tablespoons of flour and coat lightly and discard the excess. Preheat the oven to 350 degrees F. Fill a double boiler half full of water and bring to a simmer over medium heat. Using a hand held electric mixette and a metal bowl,(making sure the bowl fits over the saucepot of water) beat the whites to a soft peak. Slowly beat in the sugar. Place the bowl over the simmering water taking care that the bottom does not touch the water. Beat five minutes on a high speed until the whites become thick, shiney, and start coating the bottom of the bowl. Beat in the flour until just blended. Remove from heat, fold in the toasted chopped almonds. Drop batter by tablespoons on to the prepared buttered and floured cookie sheet. Bake thirty minutes, just until a very pale gold colour. Remove immediately to a rack and allow to cool. These cookies are best eaten within a few days, but will keep for a week in air tight container at room temperature.
